title: An AI/ML Framework for Proactive Cyber Threat Detection in Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles
authors: Oludolapo. E Ogunfowora, Aarti Panday
abstract: Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) have become indispensable across sectors ranging from logistics to defense, but their increasing reliance on software controlled cyber physical systems leaves them highly susceptible to sophisticated cyberattacks. Current cybersecurity mechanisms are predominantly reactive and fail to address emerging multivector threats such as GPS spoofing, sensor manipulation, and adversarial machine learning exploits. This paper proposes an adaptive, and protocol agnostic intrusion detection system (IDS) leveraging hybrid artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) techniques tailored for UAV environments. The framework integrates Kalman Filter driven anomaly detection with supervised and reinforcement learning algorithms, enabling continuous adaptation to evolving threats with minimal computational overhead. Blockchain based logging is integrated into the IDS pipeline to further strengthen resilience against adversarial attacks designed to deceive AI models. The framework is evaluated in a virtual UAV testbed replicating real-world flight and communication dynamics, using benchmark datasets (e.g., CSE-CIC-IDS2018) augmented with UAV specific attack scenarios. Preliminary results demonstrate improved detection accuracy and adaptability to novel threats, compared to conventional IDS solutions. The proposed framework has the potential to significantly advance proactive cybersecurity in UAV systems while remaining deployable on resource constrained platforms.

title: A Single-Motor Vibration-Driven Microrobot with Terrain Adaptability for Robust Cross-Material Locomotion
authors: Peng Liu, Chenghao Wang, Lingqi Tang, Yao Li, Bing Li
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: Vibration-driven microrobots for confined space applications (e.g., pipeline inspection, disaster relief) suffer from poor trajectory accuracy due to inherent sensitivity to surface friction variations, and conventional fixed-parameter PID controllers fail to tackle nonlinear dynamics like abrupt friction transitions and initial heading deviations-key limitations in unstructured environments. This paper presents a 2.9 g ultralight vibration-driven microrobot integrated with a fuzzy adaptive control strategy for robust locomotion on heterogeneous surfaces. Systematic experiments conducted on wood, acrylic, aluminum and cross-material composite surfaces show that the proposed control reduces the trajectory error to ~25 mm on low-friction surfaces and significantly suppresses error accumulation under cross-material transition conditions, outperforming traditional PID control. This work addresses the core challenge of friction sensitivity in vibration-driven microrobots to advance their development, and provides a solution for their reliable operation in real-world dynamic surface scenarios.

title: End-to-End Causal Inference with LLM Agents via a Structured Framework
authors: Aaron Belikoff, Matthew Lamborne
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Causal inference is the task of quantifying the effect of one variable on another. It requires both quantitative reasoning skills and common-sense causal knowledge of the world. Previous methods have attempted to utilize LLMs for causal inference, relying on their world knowledge and reasoning ability. However, it is hard for LLMs to bridge the gap between causal knowledge from the semantic information in a dataset and practical causal inference. Existing approaches directly provide information to the model on causal structure and appropriate inference method. In this paper, we propose an end-to-end approach that aims to allow LLM agents to conduct causal inference without manual annotations. We propose a 6 -step reasoning framework that encourages the model to do causal inference end-to-end. The framework operates in identification, planning, and tool calling phases, for which we integrate tools from existing causal reasoning libraries. We evaluate our framework using Qwen3-14B on QRData, a benchmark covering both numerical causal inference and multiple-choice causal discovery tasks. We also introduce a modified version of the benchmark without explicit instructions about method use or variable roles to assess end-toend capability. In general, we show that our framework is stronger, outperforming the baseline set by GPT-4 with ReAct tool calling despite using a smaller model. In particular, it is better at causal inference with limited guiding information. These findings highlight the value of structured causal reasoning frameworks in underspecified settings.

title: The Aerospace Corporation's Compendium of Recent Radiation Testing Results
authors: S. C. Davis, R. Koga, K. Lee, J. L. Taggart, G. Tzintzarov, A. W. Wright, M. A. Willhoff
publish: 2025 IEEE Radiation Effects Data Workshop (REDW) (in conjunction with 2025 NSREC)
abstract: Radiation testing was performed on several commercial components to determine the response of these components to the space radiation environment. Testing was mostly focused on SEE from protons and heavy ions.

title: TruthLens: A ResNet CNN Approach for Detecting Manipulated Media
authors: Kavyashree G M, Smitha Rai, Madhura J, Santhosh M, Prathima Mabel J, Bharath B C
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Deepfakes are falsely created images videos or audio prepared in the techniques of Artificial Intelligence (AI) exclusively in GANs(Generative Adversarial Networks). The lines between authentic and synthetic media are more hard to draw due to the level of realism portrayed by them. Deepfakes are hence associated with the spread of pseudoscience detect on-line fraud and reputational harm. TruthLens, an AI-powered deepfake recognition system, has been developed as a wide-ranging solution to analyze and endorse chromatic content using radical deep-learning approaches, explored the various approaches. A fusion manner coalescing ResNet-18 for deep three-dimensional feature taking out and an Autoencoder centered incongruity recognition contrivance to pinpoint elusive variations in facial qualities, unification provinces, illumination shapes, and gesture subtleties. Computer vision models like CNN examine spatial inconsistencies within images and videos .This model presented key progress in deep fake detection, outlines the strengths and limitations of current methods and highlights areas where continued research and innovation are urgently needed.

title: Simulating Survival in Glioblastoma: Clinical Decision Tool Based on MGMT and IDH1 Biomarkers
authors: Sarayu Ganganalli
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Glioblastoma multiforme (GBM) remains one of the most aggressive and fatal brain tumors, with a median overall survival of less than 15 months. There are biomarkers such as MGMT promoter methylation and IDH1 mutation which have been recognized as important prognostic markers for GBM; however, these measures are rarely integrated into real-life decision-making tools. The aim of this project was to build on previous work by developing a survival prediction model based on machine learning, using simulated clinical data derived from 500 synthetic GBM patients. The clinical data used for the synthetic patients included clinical characteristics such as age, sex, tumor size, KPS score, treatment type, and biomarker status. A Random Forest Regressor was used to make predictions on the time to overall survival and was deployed using an interactive app that was built using Streamlit. The synthetic nature of the dataset allows for quick simulation of survival outcomes across many potential patient profiles, allowing for a meaningful representation of the technology to link molecular data with real utilization in clinical predictive modeling.

title: Strain Engineering for Next-Generation FDSOI MOSFET, Probing CESL Relaxation with Xe, Ar, and Ge Ion Implantation
authors: L. D. Mohgouk Zouknak, B. Mohamad, K. Romanjek, L. Lachal, Z. Chalupa, M. Charbonneau, W. Vandendaele, V. Mandrillon, F. Mazen, A. Anotta, N. Gauthier, D. Mariolle, L. Brévard, L. Brunet, Ph. Rodriguez, B. Duriez, M. C. Cyrille, C. Fenouillet-Beranger
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: The complexity and cost associated with dual CESL (Contact Etch Stop Layer) integration in advanced CMOS nodes present significant challenges. CESL strain relaxation offers an attractive and cost-effective alternative. In this work, we demonstrate that an appropriate choice of implanted species and process parameters can enable the complete relaxation of a 1.4 GPa SiN tensile CESL in the pMOS region, without inducing any morphological degradation. Electrical characterization of 28 nm FD SOI nMOS reveals that Xe implantation induces the most pronounced stress relaxation and electron mobility degradation, corresponding to a full recovery of pMOS performance. Importantly, this is achieved while preserving device integrity, with no measurable impact on gate or source/drain resistances.

title:
Total Ionizing Dose Effects on 28nm Microchip RT Polarfire
                    <sup>®</sup>
                    FPGA SRAM Physical Unclonable Functions
authors: Mairely Urias, Alex Cai, Krunal Dave, Nadia Rezzak, G. Richard Newell
publish: 2025 IEEE Radiation Effects Data Workshop (REDW) (in conjunction with 2025 NSREC)
abstract: This paper provides the findings of gamma radiation effects on the Radiation Tolerant PolarFire<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">®</sup> Physical Unclonable Functions (PUF). The results support suggestions that smaller technology nodes (as shown comparing the 28 nm PolarFire FPGA to larger nodes) show higher resistance to Total Ionizing Dose (TID) changes to the power-up state of static random-access memory (SRAM) [1], demonstrating space applicability in providing radiation-tolerant security primitives used for device authentication and device-specific key generation [2]–[4].

title: Implementation of a Lightweight Compression Architecture for Real-Time UHD Video Integration on FPGA
authors: Bing-Yi Lin, Shanq-Jang Ruan, Yu-You Xie
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: High-resolution video integration is essential in real-time applications such as surgical navigation, industrial monitoring, and UHD broadcasting. However, merging multiple HD video streams into a unified 4K display remains challenging due to high bandwidth and memory demands. This work presents an FPGA-based system incorporating a low-latency, lossless compression framework. Unlike the other implementations, the proposed Zone-based Positional Compression Encoding (ZPCE) is implemented entirely in RTL and operates in a memoryless, single-pass, where compression is achieved by reducing transmitted data words while preserving a fixed width at interface. These features eliminate the need for frame buffering or complex control logic and makes it well-suited for lightweight FPGA systems. The architecture integrates four 1080p SDI inputs into a single 4K HDMI 2.0 output while supporting multiple SDI standards (3G/6G/12G). Experimental results show efficient compression with low usage and latency. This work offers a scalable solution for real-time UHD video integration across latency-sensitive domains such as medical imaging, industrial vision, and professional video systems.

title: Dynamic Optimal Power Flow for an Isolated Microgrid Using a Cheetah Optimizer
authors: Masoud Ahmadipour, Hussein Mohammed Ridha, Muhammad Murtadha Othman, Mohammad Lutfi Bin Othman
publish: 2026 IEEE 6th International Conference in Power Engineering Applications (ICPEA)
abstract: In this research, the dynamic optimal power flow (DOPF) problem for islanded microgrids integrating renewable energy sources (RES) is solved using a bio-inspired Cheetah Optimizer. The proposed algorithm emulates the adaptive hunting behavior of cheetahs through dynamic pursuit, speed modulation, and strategic resting phases, enabling an effective balance between exploration and exploitation during the optimization process. Unlike approaches that heavily rely on the current best solution, the Cheetah Optimizer conducts broad exploration in the early stages and gradually intensifies exploitation as convergence proceeds, thereby mitigating premature convergence. Its adaptive movement mechanism allows efficient handling of the nonlinear and high-dimensional characteristics of islanded microgrids. The performance of the proposed method is validated on a real islanded 15-bus microgrid network, and simulation results demonstrate its superior solution quality and convergence robustness compared with existing optimization techniques.

title: HOTP - High-conf Only Taken Predictor for Efficient Superscalar Multi-level Branch Predictor
authors: Bhawandeep Singh Harsh, Jose Renau
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on Performance Analysis of Systems and Software (ISPASS)
abstract: Branch prediction in superscalar processors faces a fundamental trade-off between prediction latency and accuracy. Ideally, a designer would like a single, very large predictor that can make multiple high-quality predictions in one cycle, but such a design only works at low frequency. Traditional multi-level or override predictors provide fast restart after mispredictions but sacrifice accuracy and effective capacity due to redundancy between the slow and fast predictors. Ahead predictors tolerate prediction latency by using older history as the predictor index. The reduced history affects accuracy, and the large predictor affects restart latency. This latency–accuracy tension limits performance in wide-issue superscalar designs. We propose a biased overriding multi-level hierarchy with three predictor levels (L0, L1, L2). L0 uses a High-Confidence-Only Taken (HOTP) predictor that only predicts high-confidence taken control-flow instructions. L1 and L2 are TAGE-based predictors sharing a bimodal table with biased updates. This design achieves fast 1-cycle restart for common branches while maintaining high accuracy for difficult predictions. Our evaluation shows that HOTP $+\mathrm{L} 1+\mathrm{L} 2$ achieves $3.4 \%$ higher IPC than TAHEAD and $3.2 \%$ higher than a traditional 2bit $+\mathrm{L} 1+\mathrm{L} 2 \mathrm{nb}$ hierarchy, demonstrating that properly designed multi-level hierarchies can surpass continuous-prediction approaches. For superscalar implementations requiring multiple predictions per cycle, we size a superscalar TAGE variant (BATAGE) using Optuna-based search with varying table sizes and aspect ratios. Combining HOTP, biased training, and this optimally sized predictor yields $3.7 \%$ IPC improvement over traditional 3-level predictors (2bit+L1+L2nb), offering concrete design options for modern open-source superscalar cores.

title: Tracked Unmanned Ground Vehicle Navigation Based on A* Algorithm
authors: Wang Wen, Liying Zhang, Siyang Wen, Yacong Hu
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Mechatronics, Control and Robotics (ICMCR)
abstract: Efficient path planning is crucial for tracked unmanned ground vehicles (T-UGVs) in complex environments, such as PV installation sites. However, traditional A* algorithms often yield unsmooth paths and ignore the unique steering characteristics of tracked platforms. This study proposes an improved A* algorithm that refines the evaluation function by incorporating a central steering cost model and a dynamic heuristic weighting scheme. Additionally, a redundant point removal technique and fourth-order Bézier curves are employed to smooth the trajectory. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method reduces search time by 66% to 98% and significantly improves path quality compared to traditional A*, Dijkstra, and ACO algorithms. The resulting paths are more kinematically feasible and stable for T-UGVs.

title: Enhanced Accuracy in Residential Security Using AI-Driven Multi-Model Face Recognition
authors: P.Kalyanasundaram, P. Meenakshi Devi, N Saravanan, Abishek M, Gokulnath S, Pranesh R
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Aim: Residential surveillance systems frequently suffer from false alarms, particularly in "No Human" scenarios caused by illumination variation, background complexity, and model uncertainty. This work aims to improve human presence validation and reduce false positives in practical home security environments using a confidence-aware adaptive multi-model face recognition framework. Materials and Methods: The study was conducted using a dataset of 1,500 images collected from public datasets and real-world surveillance-style scenes. The dataset includes images captured under different lighting conditions and background variations to represent realistic residential scenarios. The system’s effectiveness was measured using standard evaluation metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. Group 1 – Existing Method: The baseline system employed a single MobileNetV2-based convolutional neural network for classification of Admin, Intruder, and No Human categories. This approach represents conventional lightweight CNN-based surveillance systems. Group 2 – Proposed Method: The proposed system utilizes a confidence-aware adaptive multi-model framework integrating FaceNet, MobileNetV2, and EfficientNetB0. An adaptive decision fusion strategy based on model confidence scores was introduced to stabilize predictions and reduce classification uncertainty, particularly in No Human scenarios. Results: Experimental results demonstrate substantial improvement in No Human detection accuracy, classification stability, and overall system reliability compared to the single CNN baseline 88%. The proposed system achieved 98% accuracy while significantly reducing false alarms. Conclusion: The findings confirm that confidence-aware adaptive multi-model decision mechanisms enhance robustness and practical applicability of AI-based residential surveillance systems, offering a reliable solution for false alarm reduction in real-world security environments.

title: Energy Efficient Navigation of Autonomous Mobile Robots Using Euler Spiral Based Trajectories
authors: Tran Viet Thanh, Enock William, Uchiyama Naoki
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Mechatronics, Control and Robotics (ICMCR)
abstract: This paper presents a new approach to energy-efficiently navigate an autonomous mobile robot in a structured environment, based on Euler spirals and jerk limited acceleration profiles. Euler spirals, also known as clothoids, are curves that have curvature proportional to the curve length. The approach was evaluated using an novel energy consumption model of the robot, which identification and verification were conduct using dynamics model of each motor of the real robot. The constant curvature and other smoothening strategies were compared and Euler spirals were found the most energy-efficient. The results show that the proposed approach can reduce the energy consumption of the robot by 95.86% compared to the constant curvature approach.

title: Robust Stabilization for Nonlinear Networked Control Systems with Intelligent Estimator
authors: Amin Zarei, Yashar Mousavi, Saeed Tavakoli, Paweł Skruch, Pritesh Narayan, Saleh Mobayen
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Mechatronics, Control and Robotics (ICMCR)
abstract: This research explores a novel, simple, and robust control method for nonlinear networked control systems (NCSs) with unknown uncertainties in the strict-feedback form. A mathematical strategy is developed to control the output of NCSs by creating a stable error dynamic in a general form. This approach generates a list of formulas based on the state equations of nonlinear NCSs and derivatives of the system error. By calculating the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N$</tex>-th derivative according to the final state equation, a robust control command is introduced to strongly mitigate the lumped uncertainties. Additionally, an intelligent estimator is employed to approximate the nonlinear functions in this methodology using fuzzy logic systems (FLSs). The Lyapunov stability of the method is proven, and the effectiveness of this robust control approach is evaluated through an illustrative example in MATLAB software.

title: Total Dose and Single-Event Effects Testing of the ISL75054 Ultra-Low Noise LDO
authors: M. L. Campanella, W. H. Newman, N.W. van Vonno, D. Wackley, L. G. Pearce, C. E. Thomson, E. J. Thomson
publish: 2025 IEEE Radiation Effects Data Workshop (REDW) (in conjunction with 2025 NSREC)
abstract: We report the single event effects and total ionizing dose test results for the ISL75054 ultra-low noise lowdropout regulator.

title: Crop and Weed Detection in Agricultural Fields Using Few-Shot Object Detection
authors: Mohanapriya S, Dinesh K, Kumaravel T, Keerthana S, Malini R, Mounisa V
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Application of artificial intelligence has gained a significant momentum in determining the future of food industry with crop and weed detection playing a significant role in ensuring better productivity and more sustainable farming methods. Nonetheless, the vast majority of current approaches are based on big annotated datasets, which is a serious constraint in real-life applications in agriculture where such datasets are rare, and weed species are not equal across the regions. To meet this challenge, the current paper suggests a new combination of YOLOv8 with Few-Shot Object Detection (FSOD) to obtain high-quality crop and weed detection with a small number of labeled samples, that is, based on transfer learning by using pre-trained YOLOv8 models to extract discriminating visual features, i.e., shape, texture, and spatial patterns. Generalization and overfitting are alleviated by using data augmentation methods. Crops are seen as base classes in this system because they are always available, and weeds as novel classes to enable the ability to quickly adapt to newly introduced or rare species of weeds with minimum annotation. The experimental findings also prove that the proposed methodology is highly accurate in terms of detection, which proves its efficiency in the practical use in agricultural fields. In general, the YOLOv8-FSOD framework has lessened the reliance on big annotated datasets and is beneficial in the provision of effective real-time precision agriculture.

title: TensorDynamic: Bridging Application- and Instruction-Level Fault Injection for DNN Tensor Core Execution
authors: Yuxiao Jia, Euijun Chung, Huanzhi Pu, Ben Feinberg, Hyesoon Kim
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on Performance Analysis of Systems and Software (ISPASS)
abstract: Deep neural network (DNN) inference relies heavily on Tensor Core operations, which are vulnerable to transient hardware faults in computation pipelines not protected by errorcorrecting codes (ECC). Prior fault injection work has explored both application-level and instruction-level effects on DNN accuracy. However, existing application-level approaches support only coarse perturbations and do not capture hardware execution details, while instruction-level approaches lack application-level context.To address this gap, we propose TensorDynamic, an application-aware instruction-level dynamic fault injection tool for Tensor Core execution in DNN workloads. TensorDynamic enables fine-grained fault injection into MMA (matrix-multiplyaccumulate) instructions during DNN execution. Across multiple models, we show that, under the same error injection rate and severity, application-level fault injection can produce substantially different inference outcomes from instruction-level fault injection. This result underscores the need for execution-aware fault injection when evaluating DNN resilience on GPU Tensor Cores.

title: Characterization of SEEs on Gemini APU In-Memory Processors Under Heavy Ion Radiation
authors: Diego Wildenstein, Matthew Gruber, George Williams, Neil Sampson, Alan D. George
publish: 2025 IEEE Radiation Effects Data Workshop (REDW) (in conjunction with 2025 NSREC)
abstract: Processing-in-memory (PIM) devices, also known as compute-in-memory devices, can provide several benefits to space vehicles. Due to their unique architectures, they provide high-performance computation at a relatively low power usage profile. While such benefits make these processors attractive for space use, they are not the only important factor for consideration. Processors must also be tolerant to the hazards of the space environment; in particular, flight hardware must be resilient against space radiation effects. This research details the first heavy ion radiation testing of an in-memory computing architecture. In particular, we examine the robustness of the Gemini-I Associative Processing Unit (APU) to radiation-induced single-event effects (SEEs) when exposed to heavy ion radiation. By determining the frequency of upsets in different regions of the device, it is possible to characterize how faults manifest and propagate throughout different architectural elements of the device. The data gathered in this experiment will inform future fault-mitigation strategies for PIM architectures.

title: GAN-Based Detection of Synthetically Generated Satellite Imagery
authors: Vaishnav Anand, Ivan Rodriguez
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: The proliferation of artificially manipulated satellite imagery poses threats to decision-making processes across urban planning, disaster response, and national security. Zhao, 2021 [1] provides a clear documentation of how realistic manipulated geospatial data can be, and how, to the untrained eye, it is almost unnoticeable. Despite this growing threat, research targeting geospatial deepfake detection remains severely limited, with most studies fixating on facial or general image detection. This research presents a novel Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) framework designed to detect and counteract geospatial deepfakes, leveraging adversarial learning principles to develop an AI-powered defense system with the ability to distinguish authentic satellite imagery from sophisticated deepfakes. Using the SpaceNet 7 multi-temporal satellite imagery dataset with over 70,000 authentic images across different terrains, we introduced a specialized GAN architecture that we trained on a 70/30 test split, consisting of a generator network that produces <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$128 \times 128$</tex> grayscale images from 100-dimensional noise vectors, and a discriminator network that classifies imagery authenticity through the LeakyReLU activation and dropout regularization. This model was trained for 7,000 epochs using binary cross-entropy loss optimization and Adam optimizer with a batch size of 16. The experimental results demonstrated that this GAN-based detection framework achieves 88% classification accuracy across varied geospatial forgery typologies, a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 6 - 2 3 \%}$</tex> improvement over general-purpose deepfake detectors for satellite imagery. The discriminator was successfully able to identify artificial manipulations by finding subtle statistical anomalies in spectral and textural distributions that characterize synthetic content. This work recognizes and addresses a gap in the literature where geospatial deepfakes have received minimal research attention compared to other domains, though their potential impacts are far more consequential.

title: IoT-Based Adaptive Low-Power Microgrid for Villages with Ring Main Theorem
authors: Rohith S, S VIKASH, C. Gnana Kousalya, A. Pradhipa
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Rural people often experience weak electricity supply and high distribution losses so it creates a major issue for sustainable power supply. This paper proves IoT-Based Adaptive Low-Power Microgrid designed for village environments and enhanced using a Ring Main distribution topology. The system integrates solar energy generation and battery storage and low-power IoT sensor nodes that continuously monitor voltage, current, load variations, and line health across the network. Embedded and IoT regulates the processes of this real-time data and dynamically optimize the energy flow by treating as primary critical loads and applying Ring Main based routing to achieve the available system, fault acceptable range, and balanced power distribution. The proposed framework cannot automatically schedule the loads during energy-efficient periods. It also does not support energy prediction using past sensor data to detect voltage problems early. Simulation results can show improved stability, reduced transmission losses, and efficient utilization of solar and battery resources. Overall, the system provides a resilient, energy-efficient, and cost-effective solution for rural areas by providing electricity, showing that IoT, embedded systems, and the Ring Main topology can collectively enhance nextgeneration microgrid performance in village.

title: BidHub: A Flask-MongoDB based Real-Time Auction Platform
authors: Rishab Ramesh Nair, Ayush John Daniel, Hari Haran K, Gurupriya M
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: This paper presents BidHub, a web-based auction platform using Flask and MongoDB that enables secure bidding between buyers and sellers. The system provides automated auction management with user authentication via Flask-Session, MongoDB-based data persistence, and automated winner determination. Images are stored as binary data with base64 encoding. The platform demonstrates successful integration of lightweight web frameworks with NoSQL databases for operational auction systems accessible to small businesses without complex infrastructure requirements.

title: LATTA: Langevin-Anchored Test-Time Adaptation for Enhanced Robustness and Stability
authors: Harshil Vejendla
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Test-time adaptation (TTA) aims to adapt a pretrained model to distribution shifts using only unlabeled test data. While promising, existing methods like Tent suffer from instability and can catastrophically forget the source knowledge, especially with small batch sizes or challenging corruptions. We argue that this arises from overly deterministic updates on a complex loss surface. In this paper, we introduce Langevin-Anchored Test-Time Adaptation (LATTA), a novel approach that regularizes adaptation through two key mechanisms: (1) a noisy weight perturbation inspired by Stochastic Gradient Langevin Dynamics (SGLD) to explore the local parameter space and escape poor local minima, and (2) a stable weight anchor that prevents the model from diverging from its robust source pre-training. This combination allows LATTA to adapt effectively without sacrificing stability. Unlike prior Bayesian TTA methods, LATTA requires no architectural changes or expensive Monte-Carlo passes. We conduct extensive experiments on standard benchmarks, including Rotated-MNIST and the more challenging CIFAR-10-C. Our results demonstrate that LATTA significantly outperforms existing methods, including Tent, CoTTA, and EATA, setting a new state-of-the-art for self-supervised TTA by improving average accuracy on CIFAR-10-C by over 2% while simultaneously reducing performance variance.

title: > 60 GHz Antenna-in-Package with Integrated Lead-Frame Thermal Path and Low-Dk/Df Molding for Multi-Chip Systems
authors: Hao-Che Kao, Che-Sheng Yang, Po-Kai Chiu, Chih-Hao Lin, Meei-Yu Hsu, Tao-Chih Chang, Yu-Min Lin
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: A > 60-GHz antenna-in-package (AiP) module with heterogeneous PA/LNA integration was realized using a molded-cavity chip-embedding structure. Stud-bump flip-chip bonding and laser-assisted soldering enable accurate die alignment and reliable mmWave interconnection. A low-loss molding compound (k = 2.17 W/m·K, Dk = 3.10, Df = 0.0022, CTE = 11.5 ppm/°C) and a co-designed thick-Cu lead-frame heat spreader form an efficient thermal path, achieving 0.574 °C/W thermal resistance and a junction temperature of 76.87 °C. The antenna provides 10.4 dBi peak gain with 58–62 GHz bandwidth, confirming high efficiency for >60-GHz links. The proposed architecture offers a scalable AiP platform with integrated thermal management and reliable heterogeneous die packaging for mmWave applications.

title: $\text{IMA}_{i}\text{Gen}$: A Cross-Modal Image-to-Music Generation with a Non-Deep Learning Core
authors: Ellie L. Zhang, Callie C. Liao, Duoduo Liao
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: As generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) rises, music generation has nonetheless been unexplored, particularly image-to-music, and relies on deep learning methods. Existing neural-based models depend on large datasets, raising concerns about copyright infringement and increased costs for performance improvements. In contrast, we propose an innovative cross-modal Image-toMusic Artificial intelligence Generation (IMA <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }_{i}$</tex> Gen) framework powered by a novel, non-deep learning music core, minimizing copyright infringement risks by utilizing a creative approach. The framework first generates latent rhythmic features from an input image using Large Language Models (LLMs) and follows with a purely algorithmdriven music core to automatically derive musical features, constructing symbolic music from rhythmic information. In this pilot study, we developed a web tool, GenAIM, based on this image-to-music framework that generates melodies adhering to music theory and rhythmic conventions. Experimental results show that our approach achieves an average music key confidence score of 85.3%, demonstrating its ability to produce diverse, human-like compositions. Therefore, this tool serves as a reliable copilot for composers as well as entertainment, advancing AI in music generation by bridging visual and auditory art forms across modalities. Click on the web link to run our web tool, GenAIM, to generate music from images.

title: Automated Framework for Intelligent Interview Preparation and Evaluation
authors: Niveditha M, Swetha M D, M Siva Rama Krishna, Harsha Bharadwaj, Sarthak Sengupta, Vinay R Srihari
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Candidates find it challenging to recognize and strengthen their flaws because the interview preparation process frequently lacks organized feedback and individualized examination. In this paper, a unique application that uses large language models (LLMs) to offer individualized interview coaching is proposed, along with an automated framework for intelligent interview preparation and evaluation. The solution makes use of Gradio for UI creation and Ollama for local AI processing using Google's Gemma 3:1b model. The program creates role-specific interview questions in real time, offers thoughtful response recommendations, and provides thorough feedback with score systems. This system runs completely offline, guaranteeing total data privacy and having no operating expenses, in contrast to cloud-based alternatives. In order to balance creativity and relevance, the implementation uses temperature-controlled text production and supports professional responsibilities. The evaluation's findings show high user satisfaction with an average response and feedback generating time of less than 500 milliseconds. By showcasing the viability of implementing complex language models on consumer-grade hardware for useful applications, this research adds to the expanding field of accessible AI-powered educational aids.

title: Seed Classification Using Deep Learning and Image Processing
authors: K Kavyashree Karanth, Anantha Murthy, Harshitha G M
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: This work presents a machine learning system designed to classify fruit and vegetable seeds using image processing and deep learning. A custom dataset was built with seed images from different plant families, and data augmentation was applied to help the model generalise better and manage uneven class distribution. The classification model uses MobileNetV2, a lightweight convolutional neural network, which was fine-tuned on the dataset. Training was done with the Keras framework, and images were preprocessed in real time using ImageDataGenerator. To make the system easy to use, a web interface was developed with Gradio, allowing users to upload a seed image and receive instant predictions.The model consistently outperformed conventional classifiers like SVM, k-NN, and Random Forest with respect to accuracy and reliability, and it demonstrated strong performance on the validation set. The end product is a fast and easy-to-use seed classification tool that can benefit agricultural technology platforms, farmers, and researchers.

title: Co-Design of Dynamic Decoupling and Robust Control for High-Speed Magnetic Bearing Systems
authors: ZHANG Panpan, HE Yajue, SHEN Zhihuang, CAI Sijie, SHEN Aoda, HUANG Junfeng
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Mechatronics, Control and Robotics (ICMCR)
abstract: To address the degraded performance of conventional decoupling approaches induced by dominant gyroscopic cross-coupling phenomena in high-speed active magnetic bearing-supported flexible rotor systems, an eigenstructure assignment-based dynamic decoupling methodology was developed. Based on the fully decoupled model, an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{H} \infty$</tex> control synthesis was implemented and validated through comprehensive experimental testing on a dedicated high-speed test rig. Experimental results confirm that the proposed decoupling methodology effectively mitigates gyroscopic cross-coupling under extreme operating conditions. The designed <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{H} \infty$</tex> controller not only maintains stability margins at elevated rotational speeds but also demonstrates exceptional robustness, superior disturbance attenuation characteristics, and enhanced synchronous vibration attenuation performance across the entire operational range.

title: TID Induced Parametric Degradation of Commercial-Off-the-Shelf Electronics and their SEE Responses
authors: Manzar Mahmud, Benjamin Torres-Kulik, David M. Hiemstra
publish: 2025 IEEE Radiation Effects Data Workshop (REDW) (in conjunction with 2025 NSREC)
abstract: To characterize the radiation susceptibility, two commercial-off-the-shelf electronic components were tested to gamma (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }^{60} \text{CO}$</tex>) irradiation for total ionizing dose response and to 105 MeV proton irradiation for single event effects.

title: Interlayer and Band-Engineered Charge Trap Flash Enabling Low Voltage and Reliable Quad Level Operation
authors: Sanghun Jeon, Sangho Lee, Giuk Kim, Yunseok Nam, Seungyeon Chang, Jinho Ahn
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: Developing charge-trap flash that can operate reliably at low voltages while supporting quad-level storage requires both controlled charge placement and stable switching behavior throughout repeated cycling. In this study, we introduce a device architecture that combines a thin interfacial layer inside the HfZrO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</inf> stack with a charge-trap layer designed to reshape its energy-band profile. Measurements confirm that inserting the interlayer alters the internal polarization response of the dielectric, yielding a broader programmable window, lower program and erase thresholds, and improved step-wise programming characteristics. The modified charge-trap layer further contributes to stable performance by restricting excessive electron injection, resulting in more uniform program and erase transitions even after extended cycling. Collectively, these engineering approaches enable a memory window of 13.6 V, strong incremental programming efficiency, robust disturb immunity, and sustained quad-level storage. A qualitative interpretation based on switching-energy considerations suggests that the interlayer helps maintain a more uniform polarization evolution, while the engineered trap layer reduces interface-driven variation, supporting a dependable platform for future low-voltage quad-level flash technologies

title: AI-Based Risk Assessment of Alzheimer's Conversion Using Brain Age Prediction Models
authors: D. Ganesh, M. Sunil Kumar, G Shireesha, Penta Kalyan Kumar, Y Venkata Krishna Reddy, Kandula Venkata Maheshwar Reddy
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Alzheimer's Disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disease, and early identification of risk converters is imperative to intervene effectively. Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) represents an intermediate stage between normal aging and Alzheimer's disease; however, the rate of conversion varies significantly across individuals. This paper introduces a hybrid framework using AI to forecast the conversion risk to Alzheimer's Disease through brain age modelling. The study has used structural MRI scans to predict brain age through a Vision Transformer model and computes the value of the Brain Age Gap (BAG) to diagnose abnormal brain aging. Moreover, Graph Neural Networks are used to demonstrate the connectivity pattern of the brain and diagnose structural alterations at the network level. Brain age and connectivity measures, along with other clinical variables, are incorporated into the DeepSurv framework to forecast conversion risk at various time intervals from MCI to Alzheimer's Disease. Also, the proposed method has been validated on the ADNI dataset to demonstrate that the proposed hybrid model effectively forecasts risk to Alzheimer's Disease in patients with MCI.

title: A Hybrid Approach for Stock Market Sentiment and Price Movement Analysis Using Machine Learning
authors: Neeraja Veeramachineni, K. Shilpa, Soumya Sahu, Kummari Kumar, Hansini Sadak, K Bhagyasree
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: The stock prices in the stock market are subject to changes in their past trading history, as well as, the mood of investors reflected in the financial news and social media channels. The traditional models which consist of technical and fundamental factors as mainly used in forecasting future price changes disregard the important role played by the public sentiment and the psychological factors which are important in short time variations. This paper aims at providing an integrated approach to combine the concept of sentiment analysis and past information on the price movements of the stock to provide improved forecasts on the price movements of stocks. The step of natural language processing is the most suitable to evaluate the sentiment score polarity of unstructured text on social media and financial news platforms and predict it with the help of both machine learning models and deep learning models on the basis of previous information about stock price changes. The collected information of the sentiment analysis and the past is then taken to the collective forecasting model to predict.

title: Array Scale Evaluations of High-k Inside Memory Hole (HIM) Structure for Advanced Z-scaling of 3D Flash Memory
authors: Takumi Moriyama, Naohiro Hosoda, Ryota Suzuki, Masanori Tsutsumi, Shunsuke Takuma, Masashi Iwasaki, Satoshi Shimizu, Fumie Kikushima, Tatsuya Ishikawa, Tatsuo Ogura, Katsuyuki Sekine
abstract: Bit density of 3D flash memory has been improved by increasing numbers of word-line (WL) layers for vertical scaling (Z-scaling); however, this approach raises process cost. Though gate pitch scaling is essential to reduce the process cost, one of the issues for the gate pitch scaling is a deflection of dielectric between WLs (WL dielectric) during gate replacement process. In this study, a High-k Inside Memory Hole (HIM) structure at array scale was evaluated to overcome this issue, where a high-k layer is formed inside the memory hole (MH) instead of via slits. The HIM structure reduces final thickness of WL dielectric, thereby enabling further gate pitch scaling. Triple Level Cell (TLC) Vth distributions after 3k program/erase cycles were demonstrated in the HIM structure with a rectangular gate shape. High temperature data retention (HTDR) of the HIM structure were comparable to those of the conventional structure. The HIM structures enable 24% gate pitch scaling and 14% increase of the number of WL layers while maintaining total stack height. The HIM structures offer a promising solution for cost-effective Z-scaling in future 3D flash memory.

title: Materials to Systems Co-Optimization (MSCO) Modeling Platform for PPA projections from Novel Material and Process Innovations Targeting Next Generation DRAM Technologies
authors: Ashish Pal, Hojin Kim, Gregory Costrini, Ning Yang, Rohit Gupta, Subi Kengeri, El Mehdi Bazizi
abstract: We introduce a novel Materials to Systems Co-Optimization (MSCO <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${}^{\text{TM }}$</tex>) modeling platform for DRAM technology to enable array-level PPA projection from dimension scaling material and process selection, as well as architecture configuration. Our DRAM MSCO platform incorporates access transistor electrical characteristics along with cell and array-level detailed parasitic resistances and capacitances. Using our DRAM MSCO platform, we evaluate array-level PPA impact for several scenarios such as access transistor electrical properties modification as well as WL and BL material configuration for different DRAM architectures.

title: High-Efficiency Stratospheric Aerosol: Radiative Modeling of Silver-Doped Titania Nanoparticles for Solar Radiation Management
authors: Naga Sai Dhiraj Kasam
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Climate change trajectories project irreversible impacts, bringing attention to Solar Radiation Management, specifically Stratospheric Aerosol Injection (SAI). Conventional aerosols (e.g. SO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, sulphates) have efficiency limitations like short suspension and low scattering. This study evaluates Ag-doped TiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> as an alternative by simulating radiative transfer via the Rapid Radiative Transfer Model. At 130 nm particle radii and equal concentrations, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Ag}_{-1 \mathrm{O}_{2}}$</tex> outperformed the lead conventional solid phase aerosol contender (pure TiO2) in reductions of surface heating rate and net fluxes. However, ozone loss and vapor feedback may increase due to stratospheric heating. This work highlights a novel efficient aerosol for this application but SAI and novel aerosols require further studies via robust and precise modeling to reduce uncertainties in climate sensitivity.

title: A Near Zero-DC Readout Circuit for CMOS Image Sensors
authors: Tonny Do, Robert Johansson, Ruben Gomez-Merchan, Trung Thanh Nguyen
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: This paper presents a 12-bit CMOS image sensor column readout circuit that A/D converts by charging the vertical scan line (VSL) to analog supply voltage VDDH, then ramp down VSL until the pixel source follower (PSF) starts conducting current, which triggers the comparator to flip. The resolution is 1600x1200 and the column pitch is 2.625µm. The readout circuit average power consumption is 422µW at a frame-rate of 30fps with a read noise (RN) of 0.469 mV<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rms</inf>. This results in a FoM2 of 3.21 V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rms</inf> · fJ. The readout circuit has been taped out in a 22-nm CMOS process and measurements have been acquired from packaged parts.

title: Machine Learning-Based Real-Time Cardiovascular Risk Assessment for Spaceflight: A Multi-Biomarker Approach Using Inspiration4 Mission Data
authors: Rahul Gupta, Shail Gupta, M Windy McNerney
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Early cardiovascular risk detection during spaceflight requires innovative monitoring approaches that can identify physiological stress before clinical symptoms manifest. We developed CardioPredict, a machine learning-enabled point-of-care diagnostic system using biomarker data from SpaceX Inspiration4 civilian astronauts to predict cardiovascular risk in microgravity environments. Our approach addresses the unique challenges facing civilian spaceflight participants who may lack the extensive physical conditioning of professional astronauts, making them more susceptible to rapid cardiovascular adaptations. Through advanced feature engineering of inflammatory, thrombotic, and metabolic markers, our Elastic Net model achieved 97.6% prediction accuracy (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}=0.976 \pm 0.015$</tex>) with 0.52 risk unit precision. Metabolic dysregulation markers (Fetuin A) and acutephase inflammatory responses emerged as primary predictors, with temporal biomarker dynamics proving crucial for early detection. This technology platform enables the development of miniaturized lab-on-chip devices for real-time biomarker monitoring, with immediate applications for space medicine and terrestrial critical care environments where rapid cardiovascular risk stratification is essential.

title: Numerical Simulation of the Three-Phase Flow Characteristics of Solid-Liquid-Gas in the Filling Valve Based on CFD-DEM
authors: JiYangYang Ding, Junfang Ni
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: To reveal the three-phase flow pattern of solid-liquid-gas in the beverage filling valve, this paper studies the effects of particle size, flow rate and inlet angle based on the CFDDEM coupling model. The results show that particle size determines the fluid's followability; high flow rate enhances the group effect to improve the flow stability; the inlet angle controls the momentum loss, and 30° is the critical threshold for improving the flow. The research suggests that the inlet angle of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 0^{\circ}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4 5^{\circ}$</tex> should be preferred and the particle parameters should be reasonably matched to achieve efficient and stable operation of the system.

title: A Low-Cost PWM-Controlled Driver Circuit for Bioelectronic Medicine
authors: William Penhall, Dhaval Solanki
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Bioelectronic stimulation is a rapidly growing method of neural therapy. Momentary surges of low-current electrical signals can help treat psychological illnesses over time. In this paper, we developed a precise pulse width modulation (PWM)based current controller that uses both single-supply and dualsupply power rails. We successfully prototyped a design on a breadboard which included: 1) A PWM-voltage DC-DC converter and 2) a non-inverting amplifier stage. Its design was then constructed and tested on a printed circuit board (PCB) to verify precision and accuracy. Simple and space-efficient designs such as our current controller aim to offer a low-cost alternative to bioelectronic labs. The global need for this industry grows every year as drug prices surge, and funding such an endeavor becomes challenging. After careful testing, our PCB responds accurately to signals from a microcontroller, and outputs voltages reliably close to their calculated values. Measurements fall within uncertainty of the oscilloscope's voltage readings, which contain an uncertainty of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 0.5 \%$</tex>.

title: Financial Benefit of Interchangeability of Power Transformers on the Eskom Standard Plinth
authors: Malusi Mathonsi, William Lehobye
abstract: The replacement of the failed major substation equipment at National Transmission Company South Africa (NTCSA), substations are critical to maintain the South African grid code with respect to adherence to contingency plan and it must be managed within the limited time. The NTCSA transformer specification plays a key role to ensure that the same size capacity transformers are manufactured within the range of specified dimensions, and they can fit onto the plinths without any modifications alternatively, the substation design shall ensure that the plinth covers all possible scenarios, i.e., the possible future replacement with the highest dimensions. The study details the best recommended practice for the utilities to minimize such costs.The recommendation of the study is that the NTCSA specification must restrict the overall size of the transformer for each size such that the transformer from any manufacture can be installed on the standard plinth, standard bund wall and the engineering design office must design the plinth to accommodate the transformer with the highest dimensions for each capacity size. It further recommends that the panel of contractors must be appointed to be ready to address all the transformers that are about to fail based on asset health index. The study found that reasonable cost savings for more than 12% can be achieved. This can be drastically reduced by ensuring interchangeability, which emanates from the fact that there will be no civil work and design fees will be eliminated.

title: Vision-Energy Coupled Robotic Dry Ice Cleaning with Adaptive Trajectory Planning for Runway Prism Lights
authors: Kezheng Sun, Yongyong Sun, Wu Yan, Yangmin Ou, Zhaoyang Liao
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: Embedded runway lights, particularly centerline and touchdown zone lights, are critical for aircraft safety during landing and takeoff. However, the recurring accumulation of aircraft tire rubber on prism surfaces significantly degrades their optical performance. Traditional manual cleaning is laborintensive and inefficient, while existing automated systems often employ fixed-parameter cleaning, leading to resource waste and suboptimal utilization of narrow maintenance windows. This paper proposes an intelligent, vision-guided dry ice cleaning system that integrates a six-axis robotic arm with localized contamination assessment. We develop a Threshold-based Area Ratio (TAR) model to quantitatively score the cleanliness of vertically partitioned prism zones. Furthermore, an Effective Energy Flux Model (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$E_{\text {eff }}$</tex>) is established to bridge the gap between visual scores and physical cleaning intensity. Based on this, a Loop Index Compensation (LIC) algorithm is implemented for adaptive trajectory planning, enabling a “clean-on-demand” workflow. Experimental results using a ColdJet PCS 60 system demonstrate that the proposed method restores prism cleanliness to over 0.75 while reducing dry ice consumption by approximately 25 % and shortening operational time by up to 44 % compared to conventional fixed-parameter methods.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Neural Architectures for Collaborative Autonomous Intelligence
authors: Sandeep Soni, Rajvir Saini
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: HITL neural architectures have also emerged as a promising method to enhance the decision-making, flexibility, and safety of autonomous systems. Some of the difficulties that autonomous systems face are a lack of flexibility and transparency, plus the ability to adapt to unforeseeable environments. These shortcomings are especially evident in risky applications like autonomous driving, robotics, and the medical field. This study will present the new version of the HITL framework that would have assisted in overcoming these limitations by incorporating human feedback into the decisionmaking process in real-time. The architecture allows human learning through human expert participation in the strategic decision-making locations, enhanced edge case handling, and increased decision-making in dynamic and demanding environments. The system that was proposed was tested in three key areas, which were autonomous driving, robotics, and healthcare. The experimental findings indicate that the HITL system performs better than the fully autonomous system, with a decision accuracy of 92.5% as opposed to 85.0% of the autonomous system. Moreover, the HITL system demonstrates a 15% acceleration in learning rate, which is the positive influence of human feedback on the system to be able to adapt and learn. Although these results are promising, there are challenges associated with the integration of human feedback in real-time, and these challenges include feedback latency and scalability. This paper emphasizes the usefulness of HITL systems in enhancing autonomous decision-making and proposes future research directions to minimize the delays in feedback and make autonomous systems more robust and adaptable, which would result in more robust, ethical, and adaptable autonomous systems.

title: DeepLymphNet: A Deep Learning and approach for Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia Detection
authors: Minal K Deore, M Brindha
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Death due to cancer is still proceeding that lead to delayed diagnoses or missed detection. Metastasis is a significant contributor to the cancer fatality rate. Early detection of or recognition carcinogens is critical to facilitate early diagnosis and treatment. Some common cancers include breast, prostate and lung cancer and they are usually diagnosed using a variety of imaging modalities such as Computed Tomography (CT), Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) or an X-ray. Although pattern recognition techniques and diagnostic tools such as ABCD (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter) works exist these solutions might not achieve the best results. From this, there is large opportunity for healthcare automation; especially in the prediction and prevention of deadly diseases like cancer. This work presents DeepLymphNet, a concurrent deep learning based framework for classification and detection of ALL. Through employing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) using different types of activation functions such as Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU), Attention-based ReLU (AReLU), Swish, Mish and Exponential Linear Unit -ELU, the proposed model achieved a record 100% accuracy. Overall, the DeepLymphNet framework yields better results than classic diagnostic techniques and proposes a new fully automatic solution for analyzing leukocytes samples. This work shows the strong capability of deep learning techniques in medical image analysis, which provides a fast and cost-effective way to diagnosis relying on human experience that is susceptible to error. By minimising the chances of a false positive diagnosis, the new approach may speed up cancer detection and enhance survival prospects.

title: Symptom-Based Disease Prediction Using Machine Learning and Chatbot Interface
authors: B.Vijaya Mounika, Bhusa Devi, Jagarapu Greeshma, Chekka Dhilleswara Rao, Patchipulusu Mohan Sai Durga Rajeev, Polamarasetty Gnaneswar Tirumal
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: The rapid advancement of healthcare technologies has created opportunities to improve disease diagnosis and patient awareness. Early recognition of disease is often difficult in primary healthcare, especially for individuals who do not have immediate access to medical professionals. To address this issue, this study develops a symptom-based disease prediction system that uses machine learning and delivers results through a conversational chatbot. Multiple classification models, including Decision Tree, Random Forest, K-Nearest Neighbors, Naive Bayes, and Logistic Regression, were trained using a curated symptom–disease dataset enriched with severity scores and precautionary information. The system enables users to report symptoms interactively and receive probable disease outcomes along with explanatory descriptions and safety recommendations. Experimental evaluation shows that Random Forest and Decision Tree models achieve consistently high predictive performance. The proposed system is intended as an assistive and educational tool for preliminary health awareness and does not substitute professional medical diagnosis.

title: CloudJump III: Optimizing Cloud Databases for Tiered Storage
authors: Zongzhi Chen, Mo Sha, Feifei Li, Sheng Wang, Baolin Huang, Guoqing Ma, Huaxiong Song, Ke Yu, Xizhe Zhang, Yuan Wang
publish: Companion of the International Conference on Management of Data
abstract: None

title: CLAPS: A Load-Aware Proxy Resource Pooling System for Reducing Resource Redundancy in Large-Scale Cloud Storage
authors: Xiuqi Huang, Shiwei Cao, Honghao Wang, Ruijie He, Huameng Zhou, Cheng Wang, Linyu Chen, Qingsong Zhu, Shunxin Min, Yunlong Wang, Xunzhi Xu, Yangming Chen, Chen Ling, Bingwen Yuan, Yushan Sun, Maoyin Liu, Chengyu Dong, Rui Shi, Wei Chen
publish: Companion of the International Conference on Management of Data

title: SAGE: Explainable Semantic-Aware Graph-Based Entity Integration for Heterogeneous Data Sources
authors: Jaya Prakash Arjarapu, Sai Chinmai Kalakota, Radha Krishna Pisipati
publish: Companion of the International Conference on Management of Data
