title: A Hybrid GWO-XGBoost Framework for Predicting Power Outage Duration in Northern Mindanao Distribution Utility
authors: Allan Jay L. Baco, Abdul Aziz G. Mabaning, Noel Estoperez
publish: 2026 IEEE 6th International Conference in Power Engineering Applications (ICPEA)
abstract: Accurate prediction of Power Outage Duration (POD) is essential for improving distribution system reliability and customer satisfaction; however, conventional approaches often struggle to capture the stochastic and non-linear nature of restoration processes. This paper proposes a novel hybrid framework combining the Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) with eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), where GWO automates hyperparameter tuning by efficiently navigating the search space to minimize prediction error. The proposed model is validated using 325 historical outage events from a distribution utility in Northern Mindanao, Philippines, and is benchmarked against three alternative hybrid models. The results demonstrate that the GWO-XGBoost architecture yields superior predictive accuracy and significantly reduced computational time, achieving a Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) of 1.27 hours and a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.89 hours with 98.89% accuracy. Feature importance analysis identifies circuit device type and temporal operational constraints as the primary drivers of outage duration. The GWO-XGBoost framework provides a robust, data-driven basis for generating dynamic Estimated Time of Restoration (ETR) to support Smart Grid incident management.

title: HybiNet: Multi-Model Feature Fusion with Deep Explainability for Chest X-ray Diagnosis
authors: Ahmed Faizul Haque Dhrubo, Md. Khurshid Jahan, Maher Ali Rusho, Mohammad Abdul Qayum
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: In this paper, we present HybiNet, a novel hybrid deep learning model, for multi-label classification on chest Xrays, that takes advantage of EfficientNetB0, DenseNet121, and Xception with the low computational cost of SqueezeNet. By allowing for the fusion of features at an internal layer, rather than only at the output, HybiNet is able to achieve better performance, while maintaining lower computational cost. In addition, HybiNet employs multi-input joint training with a shared backbone and custom generators, allowing for better efficiency. HybiNet also provides early work blending explainability via Grad-CAM, along with bounding box verification, giving usefulness and transparency to the model. Setting a new high-water mark in medical imaging AI with training accuracy of 95.67%, HybiNet is accurately, efficient, and interpretable, while also recognizing the importance of environmental sustainability.

title: A Lightweight Instance Segmentation Network for Highly Clustered Ores Driven by Hybrid Priors
authors: Xintong Zhang, Yiran Wang, Guangpeng Zhu, Jun Wei
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: Accurate ore particle segmentation is crucial for industrial size analysis but is hindered by high-frequency texture interference and tight physical adhesion. To address these challenges, we propose OreSegNet, a lightweight instance segmentation network for highly clustered ores. We introduce a Structure-Texture Decoupling (STD) module using fixed operators as geometric priors: shallow layers employ Scharr and Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG) operators to sharpen physical boundaries, while deep layers use a Gaussian filter to suppress texture noise. A Local Feature Enhancement Attention (LFEA) module is designed to adaptively fuse these hard priors with learned semantic features. Extensive experiments on our custom dataset demonstrate that OreSegNet achieves an optimal speed-accuracy trade-off. It reaches an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$80.8 \% \text{mAP}$</tex> with only 23.56 M parameters and an inference speed of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5.6 \text{img} / \mathrm{s}$</tex>, outperforming the ResNet-50 baseline while significantly reducing computational costs. Furthermore, in few-shot scenarios using only 10 % of training data, the model exhibits exceptional data efficiency. By maintaining high precision with a lightweight architecture, OreSegNet provides a highly efficient and feasible solution for automated industrial deployment.

title: Evaluating Transfer-Learned CNNs for Adaptive Image Steganalysis and Active Defense
authors: Prashant Patil, Harshith M Bulla, Pranathi G Reddy, Basavaprabhu, Navyashree K S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Contemporary content-adaptive steganographic schemes, notably S-UNIWARD, WOW, HILL, and HUGO, strategically modify pixels in highly textured image regions, yielding imperceptible alterations that evade conventional statistical detectors. This work introduces DeepStegDetect, a modular detection pipeline that couples Spatial Rich Model residual preprocessing with fine-tuned ImageNet-pretrained convolutional neural networks—principally ResNet50—for binary cover-versus-stego classification across four embedding algorithms simultaneously. Evaluation on the BOSSBase 1.01 corpus at payloads of 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4 bits per pixel encompasses layer-freezing ablations, architectural comparisons among ResNet18/50/101 variants, Grad-CAM-based interpretability analysis, and a prototype file-monitoring honey-pot for operational deployment. Statistical significance testing confirms that the observed 51.44% accuracy exceeds random guessing (p < 0.005), yet the modest margin underscores a persistent domain gap between natural-image representations and steganographic noise signatures. The framework nonetheless offers a reproducible, extensible baseline and identifies concrete pathways—specialised architectures, larger corpora, and adversarial hardening—toward practically deployable adaptive steganalysis.

title: An 88.1TOPS/W Hidden-Neural-Network Processor for Anomalous Sound Detection
authors: Li-Yuan Chang, Ren-Hao Chiou, Heng-Siang Gao, Chia-Hsiang Yang
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: Anomalous sound detection is crucial for AI-embedded edge devices on real-time monitoring. Among neural networks, hidden neural network (HNN) enables on-chip weight generation with minimal external memory access. This paper presents an energy-efficient HNN processor for anomalous sound detection. Multicoated masking is employed to reduce the number of weights. Zero skipping enables bypassing redundant operations by exploiting the weight sparsity. Space-time speculation is applied to terminate less-significant multiply-accumulate (MAC) operations early. Overall, the computational complexity is reduced by 88.9% through algorithm-architecture co-design. In hardware architecture, data are processed in parallel to reduce the latency. A stream interface is designed to reduce the data access time by 50%. Asymmetric bitwidth allocation is leveraged to reduce the activation memory size by 33.3%. Fabricated in 40nm CMOS, the processor attains 78.3-to-93.3% accuracy in area under the curve (AUC) on the MIMII dataset, comparable to conventional DNN implementations. The chip achieves 88.1 TOPS/W, delivering 30× higher energy efficiency than the state-of-the-art HNN processor.

title: Context-Adaptive Instance Segmentation for Photovoltaic Panel Inspections
authors: Nicholas Bailey
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Affordable unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and camera payloads have made aerial photovoltaic inspections practical. Still, scalable per-panel segmentation from RGB orthomosaics remains a bottleneck for reliable defect triage and RGB-thermal data fusion. We present a zero-shot pipeline that uses the Segment Anything Model (SAM 2.1) with grid prompts derived from panel dimensions and ground sampling distance, repeated prompting on lightly sharpened image variants, and a context-informed adaptive post-processing stage. Evaluations on sections of orthophotos from five rooftop sites spanning well-lit, cross-site, low-light, and shadowed conditions show that the proposed method sustains near-perfect Precision and high recall on unseen sites, with intersection over union values of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.89-0.95$</tex>. We found that the proposed model maintains an F1-score of 0.97 or better without shadows and 0.91 with shadows, while YOLO's performance is sharply degraded on the unseen sites. Although SAM is slower per full-resolution image than YOLO, it is well suited for offline batch analysis and enables accurate zero-shot per-panel segmentation that supports robust RGB-thermal fusion for practical drone inspection workflows.

title: DaVoS: An Instantaneous Safety Model for Autonomous Vehicles
authors: Yuankai He, Yuxin Wang, Boyang Tian, Weisong Shi
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Mobility, Operations, Services and Technologies (MOST)
abstract: Reliable, interpretable measurement of autonomous vehicle (AV) safety remains a key obstacle to widespread deployment. In practice, industry-facing summaries emphasize exposure-normalized outcomes (e.g., crashes per mile), while research metrics such as time-to-collision (TTC) provide scenario-level kinematic diagnostics; both are valuable but do not yield a single physics-grounded scalar that exposes the coupling between predicted conflicts and the distance traveled during nonzero sense–decide–act latency. This paper proposes DaVoS, a physics-based, platform-agnostic model for evaluation: an instantaneous formulation that summarizes safety confidence under the current plan and predictions using five measurable quantities—distance to first predicted conflict along the ego planned trajectory, current speed, longitudinal acceleration/deceleration, end-to-end sense–decide–act overhead, and a variability descriptor that selects conservative derating. The DaVoS model allows a vehicle to calculate a safety-confidence score at any instantaneous time from these metrics. DaVoS compares conservative available distance to conservative committed distance over the overhead window and maps the resulting slack to a bounded score. The metric is intended for comparison on matched scenario sets under a disclosed conflict predicate, horizon, conservative policy, and score calibration. We illustrate the score on two highway scenarios (car-following and an adjacent-lane cut-in) and provide measurement guidance for reproducible reporting.

title: Process Selection Strategy for Chiplet-Based 2.5D Systems under Design Constraints
authors: Zi-Wei Huang, Yi-Ting Li, Wuqian Tang, Liang-Chia Cheng, Yung-Chih Chen, Chun-Yao Wang
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: As monolithic SoC designs face growing performance limitations, heterogeneous 2.5D integration has become a practical alternative, which enables efficient assembly of multiple chiplets, that may use different process technologies. Selecting a suitable process technology for each chiplet is critical for minimizing cost and satisfying constraints on area, power, and thermal issues. In this paper, we propose an approach that efficiently determines the optimal process selection for each chiplet in a heterogeneous 2.5D integration design. Our approach reduces computation time by over 90% on average for a set of benchmarks compared to conventional dynamic programming, while achieving high-quality, constraint-compliant designs.

title: A Robust Rag-Based Pipeline for Automated Medical Conversation Transcription and Validation
authors: Aarav Balaji, James Finch, Andrew Breithaupt
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Healthcare systems worldwide maintain vast databases of electronic health records (EHRs). They allow for a standardized way of accessing vital patient information across different healthcare providers and hospitals. However, the creation of such clinical documentation places an immense burden on clinicians. They spend extensive hours each workday maintaining EHRs, writing documentation, and recording patient data, time that could instead be devoted to patient care. Consequently, the burden of EHRs is frequently cited as a source of widespread physician burnout. Advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) and Automated Speech Recognition (ASR) are increasingly being explored in research to alleviate this burden and enhance documentation quality. However, previous work is limited and notes challenges such as model hallucination, high diarization error rates (DER), and data privacy concerns. This paper introduces a robust, Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)-compliant pipeline for automated medical conversation transcription and for validating the generated transcriptions. The pipeline leverages a hybrid transcription model: AWS Transcribe for speech-to-text generation and Pyannote.audio, an open-source, neural-network based toolkit for speaker diarization. To ensure privacy of Protected Health Information (PHI), Pyannote is executed locally on dedicated hardware and AWS Transcribe is configured to be stateless. The system further incorporates a comprehensive validation pipeline, featuring an LLM enhanced with a custom RAG framework for quantitative and qualitative analysis. Several key metrics were also generated to assess overall transcription fidelity. Our validation on mock medical conversation scenarios shows an estimate of a 300 % reduction in overall time spent on creating and maintaining EHRs, a DER ranging from 0.02 % to 8 %, word error rates (WER) from 4 % to 11 %, and MEDCON F1scores from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 4 \%}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 2 \%}$</tex>. This pipeline therefore offers a promising technique for reducing physician burnout by streamlining the process of medical documentation, ultimately allowing healthcare professionals to dedicate more time to highquality patient care.

title: Power Ranger: A Comprehensive Framework for GPU Power Characterization
authors: Allison Seigler, Zhixing Jiang, Lizy Kurian John
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on Performance Analysis of Systems and Software (ISPASS)
abstract: As GPUs have grown more popular and become commonplace in datacenters, GPU power consumption has become a more important metric than ever before. Power behavior should be a major consideration in all stages of GPU design, development, and deployment. However, accurate power evaluation is difficult because modern-day GPU workloads are large and complicated, and rapid advances in popular datacenter workloads means that the power profiles of these workloads change often. To solve this problem, we introduce Power Ranger, a GPU power benchmark generator that can replicate power behavior from any arbitrary workload using only a reference device and a trace of power over time. Power Ranger’s generated workloads replicate real workload behavior using simple kernel launches selected from a look-up table. Because Power Ranger only requires a power trace and device, and does not require any code from the target workload, it can also create real GPU workloads that match any desired power behavior. This capability enables the generation of custom power benchmarks and stress tests that can be executed on real GPU hardware. We evaluate Power Ranger using several real and synthetic workloads, and we find that Power Ranger is able to reproduce original power traces with a MAPE of $5 \%$ or less for all cases.

title: The Recycle Disciple: A Machine Learning-Powered Application for Resin Identification Code Classification and Simplified Recycling
authors: Ayesha Faruki
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Plastic pollution, as well as contamination of recycling plants with seemingly harmless non-recyclables, continues to pose a severe threat to environmental sustainability, in part due to consumer confusion around Resin Identification Codes (RICs) used to identify recyclable plastic types. This paper presents The Recycle Disciple, a mobile application powered by machine learning that classifies RICs using image inputs and provides real-time recycling guidance to users. Three models-Google's Teachable Machine, MobileNetV2, and ResNet18-were evaluated for their performance on an augmented dataset of RIC images. The ResNet18 model achieved the highest accuracy (0.86), precision (0.91), and F1-score (0.81), and was integrated into the final mobile application. The app was developed using React Native for cross-platform functionality, with a Flask backend handling model inference. This work demonstrates how lightweight deep learning models can support sustainable recycling practices through accessible technology, particularly by empowering users with immediate, reliable identification of plastic types.

title: High-Speed Real-Time Illumination Enhancement on FPGA Using Optimized Parallel Architecture
authors: Sai Chalapathi Rowthu, Kumar C, Jitendranath Reddy B, Jagan C, Jayanth N
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Real-time low-light image enhancement is an important requirement for embedded vision systems, such as surveillance, autonomous vehicles, and medical imaging. Traditional image enhancement processing, such as histogram equalization and software-based Retinex algorithms, tends to introduce artifacts or have high computational complexity. Although deep learning-based approaches are highly efficient, they require large amounts of training data and GPU-level computing, which is not suitable for real-time FPGA-based systems. This paper proposes a high-speed FPGA-based illumination-adaptive image enhancement system using an optimized parallel architecture. The proposed system decomposes the input image into illumination and reflectance images using a Retinex-inspired model and performs adaptive logarithmic image enhancement. The algorithm is efficiently implemented on FPGA hardware using multi-pixel parallel pipelines developed in Xilinx Vivado. The experimental results show that the proposed system effectively enhances the image with a Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) of 16.34 dB and a Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM)of 0.868, which is of high perceptual quality and structural preservation. Compared to traditional and deep learning-based approaches, the proposed system has lower latency and reduced hardware complexity, making it highly suitable for embedded vision systems.

title: An Intelligent Career Guidance Framework Integrating Multiple Intelligences Theory and Ensemble Learning
authors: N V Muralikrishna Raja, Vijayagiri Leela Prasad, Kolagotla Venkateswara Reddy, Senthil Kumar A, Vinod Babu Polinati, C P Pavan Kumar Hota
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Career selection is a significant problem for post-secondary education students, especially because of the inability to create data-driven and personalized career guidance systems. Traditional methods of counseling usually depend on personal opinion and do not provide a systematic evaluation of the cognitive strengths possessed by an individual; this results in most cases in career misfits and dissatisfaction with a job. To solve this problem, the proposed paper will suggest an intelligent career guidance system that would combine multiple intelligence theories and machine learning to provide personal career recommendations. The suggested system involves a well-organized 36-item test to measure cognitive and behavioral qualities of students that are further converted with the help of an Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) classification model. The model is trained and evaluated using a labeled dataset built based on student response to assessment. As the experimental findings show, the proposed solution is able to provide a good level of reliable classification performance with balanced values of accuracy and F1-score, which explains why the model can be used to project cognitive profiles to the appropriate academic and career trajectories. The system is adopted in the form of a web-based application, which allows real-time evaluation and recommendation. The results indicate that the given framework may be used as a viable decision-making device to help the students make informed and individual career decisions, specifically in the field of education where professional career guidance is less accessible.

title: Characterizing Machine Learning Force Fields as Emerging Molecular Dynamics Workloads on Graphics Processing Units
authors: Udari De Alwis, Benjamin E. Mayer, Thomas J. Ashby, Maria Barrera, Timon Evenblij, Joyjit Kundu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on Performance Analysis of Systems and Software (ISPASS)
abstract: Molecular dynamics (MD) simulates the time evolution of atomic systems governed by interatomic forces, and the fidelity of these simulations depends critically on the underlying force model. Classical force fields (CFFs) rely on fixed functional forms fitted to experimental or theoretical data, offering computational efficiency and broad applicability but limited accuracy in chemically diverse or reactive environments. In contrast, machine learning force fields (MLFFs) deliver near-quantum-chemical accuracy at molecular-mechanics cost by learning interatomic interactions directly from high-level electronic-structure data.While MLFFs offer improved accuracy at a fraction of the cost of quantum methods, they introduce significant computational overhead, particularly in descriptor evaluation and neural network inference. These operations pose challenges for parallel hardware due to irregular memory access, minimum data reuse and inefficient kernel execution.This work investigates the hardware performance of such models using poly-alanine chains, a novel benchmark molecule system(s) with controllable input size, which used as performance evaluation test cases highlighting the computational bottlenecks of the graphical processor units when scaling out MLFF simulations. The analysis identifies key bottlenecks in descriptor and force computation, memory handling, highlighting the opportunities for improvements in the emerging area of MLFF based MD in drug discovery, that has received limited attention from a computer architecture perspective.

title: Programmable Reactive Power Injection for DG System Using Statcom for Voltage Control
authors: Vijayshree G, Supreeth Kumar S, Rudresh K, Manoj S Gowda
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: This paper presents a MATLAB simulation based STATCOM (Static Synchronous Compensator) for reactive power compensation and voltage regulation in a three-phase power system. The developed model consists of a three-phase source feeding a nonlinear load, with source and load measurement blocks employed for real-time monitoring of electrical parameters. A voltage source converter (VSC) based STATCOM is connected at the point of common coupling (PCC) through an interfacing inductor and LC filter network to mitigate harmonics and ensure smooth current injection. The STATCOM control strategy dynamically regulates reactive power exchange by adjusting the magnitude and phase angle of the converter output voltage, enabling fast compensation of voltage deviations and load-induced disturbances. Simulation results demonstrate effective improvement in voltage profile, reduction in reactive power burden on the source, and enhanced system stability under varying load conditions. The observed transient behavior during system energization highlights the impact of DC-link charging and synchronization dynamics, while steady-state results confirm that the source supplies only the net active power demanded by the load. The proposed simulation framework serves as a reliable platform for analyzing STATCOM performance and can be extended to incorporate advanced intelligent control techniques for power quality enhancement in distributed generation systems.

title: Semantic Analysis and Knowledge-based Approach for Identifying right sense on Kannada text
authors: Rajanishree M, Rajeshwari K, Swetha M D, Vinay R Srihari, Madhumala RB, Jayanth C
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: The proposed research work employed Kannada, one of the South Indian languages, to develop a Natural Language Processing (NLP) tool that can comprehend sentences and at the same time, identifies the true sense for polysemous words if they are present in the sentence. The two main goals in natural language processing (NLP) are semantic analysis and word sense disambiguation (WSD), both of which are difficult to accomplish accurately. The three grammatical agreements— subject-verb-number, subject-verb-gender, and subject-verb-tense—in the sentence are the focus of a rule-based method in our research work for semantic parsing Kannada texts. With the help of the semantic analysis created in this project, users can correctly read simple Kannada sentences by considering if the statement makes sense. Sentences with a word count of no more than five are taken into consideration for parsing. Next, another NLP work, WSD, has been implemented using a knowledge-based approach. The Kannada Wordnet database was acquired from the Indo Wordnet website, which was developed by IIT Bombay. There are only 38575 Kannada words available in the Kannada wordnet. To ascertain the target word's correct sense, information has been gained straight from the website using the Selenium tool. The three techniques utilized for WSD are the Longest Common Subsequence (LCS), the Jaccard Index, and maximum overlap (intersection). We are able to determine the correct sense for every target word with encouraging precision, recall, and F1-score outcomes.

title: Enhancement of Reactive Power Support Capability in Grid-Forming Converters Under Circle Limitation
authors: Wenjie Song
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: Grid-forming (GFM) converters play a critical role in modern power systems, and their dynamic behavior during grid faults-particularly reactive power support-directly affects system stability. This paper identifies a shortcoming in the reactive power support capability of GFM converters when a circular current limiting strategy is activated during faults. To overcome this limitation, a power angle constraint method is proposed. Based on a virtual synchronous generator (VSG) model, the power-angle characteristics under the circular current limiting strategy are analyzed for different fault conditions, and analytical power expressions are derived for both constant voltage and constant current operation modes. An angle saturation control scheme is then introduced to limit the variation range of the power angle, effectively reducing the reactive power drop. Simulation results confirm that the proposed method not only improves reactive power support under various voltage sag conditions but also enhances active power recovery performance.

title: Evaluating the Effect of Exomoons on Planetary Axial Tilt Stability and Exoplanet Habitability
authors: Daksh Challa
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: This study explores the role of exomoons in influencing the axial tilt (obliquity) stability of potentially habitable planets, with a focus on understanding how lunar characteristics such as mass, semi-major axis, and orbital eccentricity affect long-term climate regulation. Using the N-body integrator REBOUND, I simulated a simplified three-body solar system, consisting of a Sun, Earth, and a moon with varying parameters, over a one-million-year period. Axial tilt variation was measured by fitting a 7th-degree polynomial to each simulation and calculating its range through its derivative. The simulations were applied to Kepler-452 b to evaluate whether these dynamics extend to real exoplanets. Four cases were tested: a moonless scenario, a control trial with moon parameters scaled from Earth, a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\frac{3}{4}$</tex> lunar mass trial, and a reduced semi-major axis trial. Results showed that removing the moon did not necessarily lead to instability, and a moonless Earth can still maintain tolerable tilt oscillations. However, decreasing the moon's semimajor axis or increasing its mass led to greater instability, likely due to enhanced gravitational torque. We also found that certain specific lunar parameters cause instability, likely due to spin-orbit resonance. The trials with the exoplanet supported these results. The study highlights the need to consider exomoon characteristics when assessing exoplanet habitability, as they may play a role in the exoplanet's axial tilt through extreme gravitational influence and spin-orbit resonance.

title: Understanding Climate Conversations: Insights from Topic Modeling of Scientific and Social Data
authors: Joysh Jayaron Jose, Getzi Jeba Leelipushpam Paulraj, Stewart Kirubakaran S, Immanuel Johnraja Jebadurai
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: In this paper, a comparison between three major topic modeling methods were done-Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF), and BERTopic-using the same text dataset to see how well they extract clear and distinct topics. The project started by preprocessing the data thoroughly: tokenizing, removing stopwords, and lemmatizing, to keep everything consistent for a fair comparison. To measure how each model performed, the focuse was on two main metrics: perplexity (which reflects how well the model generates consistent topics) and topic diversity (the spread of unique words across topics). LDA reached a perplexity of 912.3, so it handled generalization reasonably well. NMF struck a balance between being easy to interpret and offering decent diversity, scoring 71.8%. But BERTopic clearly led the pack, hitting 84.2 % for topic diversity and uncovering topics that were both semantically richer and more distinct in context. When the results were visualized, BERTopic's topics stood apart—not just separated clearly, but also more coherent. These results really drive home a shift happening in topic modeling: moving from traditional statistical methods to newer, embedding-based approaches. Transformer-powered models like BERTopic aren't just buzz-they genuinely improve how we capture and understand the themes running through text.

title: Automatic Optical Fiber Alignment for a Free Space Optical Link
authors: Aimee Linebarger, Justine Haupt
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: An underground fiber-based quantum network currently connects Brookhaven National Laboratory and Stony Brook University. Brookhaven's goal is to enhance the network by developing a system to detect or distribute single entangled photons across a 21-km free space optical link. Since the new link is through free space, it uses telescopes instead of fibers. The light sent through the system must couple to a single mode fiber whose opening is only 5 microns in diameter. The beam, having been corrected for atmospheric effects by an adaptive optics system, will have residual drift over time that must be corrected for continuously, and thus a precise and automatic fiber alignment system is needed as part of the active optics system. An alignment algorithm was developed, which will adjust the optical fiber using a three-axis translation stage, controlled by a piezo controller attached to a Raspberry Pi. Testing was conducted in the Instrumentation Division at Brookhaven. This automatic alignment system was also used for a quantum astrometry experiment at the Center for High Angular Resolution Astronomy Array at Mount Wilson, California. The final system consisted of a coarse alignment algorithm that attempted to find the focal point of a fiber coupling lens mathematically by scanning cross sections of the beam's intensity, and a fine alignment algorithm that searched a small volume of points around the previously calculated point. The automated system was as good at finding best focus as a human, and achieved a coupling efficiency greater than 50%, which was the desired outcome.

title: Robustness-Oriented Explainability Analysis of the PHQ-9 Depression Scale Using Ensemble Learning Under Response Perturbations
authors: Indira S, S. S. Ittannavar, Jagadish S Jakati, Bhavaling Alias Pratima Khot, Mahadevaswamy S, B P Pradeep Kumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Reliable assessment of depression is essential for early identification of high-risk individuals, yet psychometric instruments such as the Patient Health Questionnaire-9 are vulnerable to response variability and uncertainty. This study aims to analyze the robustness, redundancy, and explainability of PHQ-9–based high-risk classification rather than propose a predictive diagnostic system. The objectives are to examine structural stability of the scale, evaluate effects of realistic Likert-scale perturbations on clinically relevant metrics, and assess resilience of high-risk detection under increasing uncertainty. An Extreme Gradient Boosting classifier was trained using clean PHQ-9 responses, with binary high-risk labels derived from established clinical thresholds. Robustness was evaluated by introducing bounded ±1 Likert-scale noise into symptom responses at multiple perturbation levels during testing. Results show that perfect performance under noise-free conditions reflects deterministic scale construction, while increasing perturbation produces gradual degradation in accuracy and sensitivity. Crucially, the ability to detect high-risk individuals remained above 97%, even when response variability increased, while the rate of missed cases rose only slightly. This trend suggests that the PHQ-9 survey possesses inherent internal consistency and structural stability, ensuring dependable risk categorization even when self-reported answers vary. The findings highlight the value of stress-testing assessments through response variation—a practical and clinically relevant way to verify the reliability of mental health tools. Ultimately, this approach advocates for the cautious interpretation and functional use of PHQ-9 screening in routine medical practice.

title: Picosecond Probing of Selector-Only Memory Revealing a Memory Window in the Time-Domain
authors: Rivka-Galya Nir-Harwood, Taras Ravsher, Daniele Garbin, Ishay Braitbard, Eilam Yalon
abstract: We demonstrate ultrafast operation of SiGeAsSe selector-only memory (SOM) devices, achieving thresholdswitching with voltage pulses as short as ∼45 ps full width at half maximum. This establishes a new speed benchmark for SOM technology and reveals intrinsic picosecond-scale dynamics governing Ovonic threshold switching (OTS). Using this capability, we implement ultrafast programming and timedomain readout with sub-ns pulses down to 400 ps. Alternatingpolarity pulse sequences program the device into distinct polarity-dependent states, which are read out as well-separated OTS delay times. Enabled by the picosecond temporal resolution of our measurements, the resulting time-domain memory window spans 20-80 ps depending on pulse width. These results establish SOM as a viable platform for ultrafast memory operation.

title: Climora: A Deep Learning–Based Environmental Intelligence Platform
authors: Suman Kumar, Pranchal, Bhanupriya Gautam, Er. Abhishek Tiwari
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: However, the growing world-wide effects of climate variabilities, environmental degradation, and extreme weather events have amplified the requirement for the development of intelligent platforms that can efficiently utilize the capabilities of deep learning theories for the interpretation of intricate environmental information. This article discusses the development of a new deep learning-based environmental intelligence platform named Climora. This climate-related intelligence solution is designed to utilize the capabilities of deep learning theories for the integration of multi-source climate information. This climate information can be procured through the utilization of climate trend analysis, anomaly detection information included in air quality measurement systems, as well as the use of environmental risk forecast information included in space image sources. The proposed approach of deep learning and rule-based environmental intelligence ensures the precise output of climate forecast information. This research addresses important gaps in the current state of research. For example, there is a need for the development of integrated AI-supported climate information systems. It is necessary to effectively integrate heterogeneous climate data sources. Furthermore, this research fulfils the requirement for providing real-time climate forecasts.

title: Signalling and Waveform Design for Visible Light Communications
authors: Tilahun Zerihun Gutema, Wasiu O. Popoola
abstract: This article discusses approaches to enhance the efficiency of visible light communication (VLC) systems. One method explored in this study is the optimisation of the LED DC bias point, to increase the modulation bandwidth, thereby improving data transmission capacity. This technique is combined with probabilistic shaping (PS), which adapts symbol distributions to channel characteristics, improving spectral efficiency and channel capacity. Additionally, an energy-efficient frequency-spreading chirp modulation (FSCM) scheme is presented for low-power, low-data-rate VLC systems. The FSCM technique has resilience to bandwidth limitations or frequency selective channels, making it particularly advantageous for energy-constrained applications such as the Internet of Things (IoT).

title: Data Driven Risk Scoring for Progressive Kidney Dysfunction Using Ensemble Machine Learning
authors: C. Selvarathi, B Manohar Reddy, P Mohan Varma, P. Pandiaraja, Charles Prabu V, S. Maheswari
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Chronic kidney disease represents a progressive condition affecting about 10 % of the world population and requires early detection to prevent its advancement to end-stage renal failure. The current research describes a comprehensive machine learning framework for data-driven risk scoring of progressive kidney dysfunction based on routine clinical and laboratory measurements. Real challenges faced during clinical practice include mixed feature types, substantial missing values, and small sample sizes. A dataset containing 400 patient records with 24 clinical attributes, including demographics, vital signs, laboratory tests, and comorbidity indicators, was extensively analyzed in the present study. This proposed approach adopts strong preprocessing strategies, where imputation by random sampling is used for those features which have large missing data, and mode imputation is applied to categorical variables. Ten different supervised classification techniques have been employed, which include various instance-based, tree-based, and ensemble techniques that have been tested under identical settings. The results have been analyzed and suggest that ensemble techniques that include boosting, such as AdaBoost with a test accuracy of 99.17 %, CatBoost and LightGBM of 97.50 %, and Stochastic Gradient Boosting of 96.67 %, perform best. Feature importance plots revealed that hemoglobin, specific gravity, creatinine, and albumin are the four dominant predictive factors, which is correct and valid according to accepted medical standards. This approach and application primarily focus on probabilistic risk quantification and not simply diagnosis, implying that the developed clinical support system is supplementary and not a replacement for the opinion of a human medical practitioner. This study validates that strong ensemble techniques are capable of detecting meaningful patterns using small and noisy medical data sets that help identify early intervention strategies for chronic kidney disease.

title: Adaptive Impedance Control of a Dexterous Hand for Ground Assembly of Large Space Truss Structures
authors: Wenqiang Xian, Yan Wang, Jianchao Han, Yun Li, Rongrong Yu
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: To address the difficulty of controlling grasping force during the assembly of large-scale space truss flexible composite rods, this paper proposes an adaptive grasping force control method for a dexterous hand based on conventional impedance control. Considering the contact characteristics in the assembly process, a dynamic interaction model is established, and an adaptive impedance control framework is designed to achieve compliant interaction. Simulation results demonstrate that, compared with traditional impedance control, the proposed method improves system stability and force control accuracy.

title: Digital Twin for Network Energy Consumption
authors: Andy Garcia, Hanshal Chandrashekara Reddy
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: With the increasing demand for 5G and 6G in cellular networks it has led to an increase in energy consumption and operating expenses (OPEX). Creating a digital twin model will simulate network load to identify traffic patterns. Implementation of energy-saving actions, such as sleep mode on O-RAN (Open Radio Access Network) components, will determine which units can be turned ON/OFF depending on network traffic to reduce energy usage.

title: Web Server Log Analyzer Using Apache Spark and Hadoop
authors: C. Venkata Siva Sai Raju, K Subhash Chandra, P Mithun Karthikeya, Manju Venugopalan
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: As we can see that web servers expel a large amount of log data nonstop in daily life, putting every request that hits them. And breaking up those logs data is not just for hardcore system part it is key part for catching security issues, where web server shows what page, you are in, which services users visit the most by keeping system and things running without crashes or slowdowns. For this problem we created a dashboard where it handles large data using Spark for processing the log files and Hadoop for storing the log data. Where this log data is collected, cleaned and stored in a specific format, and analyzed to find abnormal activity. These results are stored in the csv, flowcharts, bar graphs are shown in the dashboard. We run the log data almost to 10 million requests from among 250,000 Ips address. Where our dashboard system shows the most popular endpoints. We’re looking to add better future improvements to make the system smarter and more advanced.

title: A Hybrid IoT Conversation Gadget That Uses Patient-Response Monitoring and a Neckband Sensor to Discover Movement
authors: Logeswaran A, Dharshini K, Madhumitha C, Manisha S, Deepika V, Keerthana N V
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: The method gives an IoT-based emergency alert gadget created to help patients who can't pass or communicate, especially those tormented by Locked-in Syndrome. The device combines three distinct detection methods to understand signs and signs of misery: Sustained eye consciousness, unusual breathing interest, and mild neck muscle motion. The MPU6050 sensor is used to stumble on weird breathing patterns, at the same time as an infrared digital camera video display gadgets constant eye focus for more than ten seconds. Moreover, a wearable neckband prepared with surface EMG or stress sensors picks up on minor muscular responses, along with swallowing or neck tension. A Raspberry Pi acts as the compute hub, processing all statistics from all sensors in real-time. For detection, employees use a hybrid deep learning technique that combines a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a longtime period reminiscence listing network (LSTM) to extract spatial capabilities from eye motion and EMG signals, and the LSTM techniques the temporal collection to capture persistent strange behavior over the years. The pair is a gadget that efficiently identifies proper genuine distress signals in noisy or random environments with high precision. The processed statistics are transmitted to the cloud with IoT protocols like MQTT. Caregivers collect without delay communications via cell indicators or virtual dashboards if there may be an issue with the patient. The device works all day and all night, or maybe in a cloudy environment. Its price-effectiveness and adaptability make it an appropriate answer for houses, hospitals, and senior care facilities. Through the usage of multiple entry assets, the gadget will increase reliability and ensure that signals can still be prompted if one method failsmaking it a reliable tool for protective individuals with severe communication obstacles.

title: Automatic Generation of UML Diagrams from refined Universes of Discourse with ABERTIN
authors: Andry Bertin, Trésor Fanomezantsoa Donazy, Andrianjaka Miary Rapatsalahy, Sorin Ilie, Raft Nicolas Razafindrakoto, Bădică Costin
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Natural Language Processing (ICNLP)
abstract: The software development process in requirements engineering (RE) always begins with the collection of user needs. Indeed, system contexts are described in the Universe of Discourse (UofD). The automatic extraction of needs hidden within the UofD is a crucial challenge. However, natural language requirements are often ambiguous, vague, and incomplete. These constitute a handicap for the construction of a UML model, which is the model used for the technical implementation of requirements. In this article, we aim to automatically extract the requirements formulated in natural language in the UofD in order to constitute the elements of a UML model. This approach leverages Natural Language Processing (NLP), particularly the camemBERT transformer model, to identify and extract requirements. It then relies on a knowledge base provided by RAG to generate and enrich the requirements. Finally, the method incorporates guided human intervention, essential for refining and validating the structured information within ReLEL. The combination of these approaches in the Augmented BERT In Needs (ABERTIN) workflow demonstrates that interactive structural validation is more effective in terms of performance and accuracy of automatic UML generation.

title: Techno-Economic Evaluation of Solar PV Generation Plant-UNISA Case Study
authors: Lehlohonolo Mojaki, Nhlanhla Mbuli
abstract: To embark on a Small-Scale Embedded Generation Solar project with confidence, the investor must be assured that the project will be viable, hence engineers and designers must understand the economical evaluation of projects. Techno-economic evaluation methods assist to bridge this gap; however, the available tools are in software packages, creating dependency and additional costs. This study aims at providing researchers and interested parties with a guide on how to achieve this from first principles, using Microsoft Excel to develop a financial model that gives the Net Present Value, Payback Period and the Internal Rate of Return. The authors used irradiance data and the Liu and Jordan method as critical inputs in the energy calculations, that laid a foundation to this publication.

title: TaintMonkey: Dynamic Taint Analysis of Python Web Applications Using Monkey Patching
authors: Carter Chew, Aarav Parikh, Shayan Chatiwala, Aiden Chen, Sebastian Mercado, Benson Liu
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Web application vulnerabilities have become increasingly common, creating a demand for robust and scalable detection tools. Current state-of-the-art (SOTA) tools experience problems with many false positives due to limitations of approaches like static analysis. Additionally, many of these tools are designed for compiled languages, whereas interpreted languages are more widely used in web applications. To address both of these issues, we introduce a novel approach that combines the ideas of dynamic taint analysis and fuzzing to discover vulnerabilities in Python web applications. We leverage monkey patching, a feature of interpreted languages like Python, allowing developers to instrument critical functions in web applications to help track the flow of insecure data at runtime without modifying the original source code. To improve the completeness of this analysis, we incorporate semantic fuzzing to explore various code paths and catch whenever a potential security violation has occurred. This paper also introduces an implementation of this approach in a dynamic taint analysis library called TaintMonkey that targets web applications using the Flask framework. To test this tool, we introduce the JungleGym dataset, a collection of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$100+$</tex> Flask web applications written in Python that contain vulnerabilities classified by the Common Weakness Enumeration (CWE). TaintMonkey was able to detect all vulnerabilities within JungleGym, demonstrating the effectiveness of this approach and the versatility of monkey patching as a practical method for detecting vulnerabilities in web applications.

title: Dual-Prompt Learning for Readability Assessment of Ultra Long Texts
authors: Qinghan Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Readability assessment of ultra long texts has a wide range of needs in educational applications. Recent progress focuses on short or standard-length passages by handling them with a one-time encoding process, but failing to encode book-level long texts due to the length limits and consumption issues of pre-trained models. In this paper, we propose TTDP, a Two-stage Training framework with Dual-Prompt representation, to handle ultra long texts effectively. Specially, we design a two-stage training paradigm to learn book representations through consistent token-to-segment and segment-to-book stages, and we devise a dual-prompt mechanism to obtain a better readability representation, by combining a set of random-initialized global difficulty prompts with text-extracted private linguistic prompts. With the proposed model, we manage to process ultra long texts using a vanilla pre-trained model, and the resource consumption is well controlled by inheriting parameters between the two stages and tuning prompts only. In addition, we construct a new dataset, Graded Children's Book, tailored for evaluating model performance on ultra long text RA. Experimental results show that TTDP achieves promising results, substantially surpassing traditional methods and pre-trained methods capable of processing long texts, as well as outperforming the current SOTA model on RA.

title: Down sampling High-Resolution Air Quality Maps Using AI/ML Approach
authors: Tabassum Ayesha HR, Swati CK, Sushma Patil, Varsha GA, Supreeth S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Air pollution severely threatens public health in urban India, where PM2.5, PM10, NO2, SO2, CO, and O3 routinely exceed CPCB limits by 20-50%. This paper proposes an end-to-end framework preprocessing multi-city historical data through cleaning (95% duplicate removal), city-median imputation, , downsampling, and feature engineering, followed by Random Forest regression validated via 5-fold cross-validation. It achieved a 20% lower MAE (15.67 μg/m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>) and 12% better R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> (0.76) for PM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2.5</inf> than Jafri et al. (MAE=21.96, single-city), 15% RMSE improvement over IEEE comparative (multi-model average.), and 25% higher AUC (0.879) than those of Sharma et al. hybrids. Unlike Ayesha et al. Gaussian processes (no deployment) or 2024 nationwide ML (basic imputation), ours integrates spatial dashboard reducing health-risk misclassification by 18%. These gains validate RF's 28% edge in noisy data handling for policymaker/citizen use.

title: Towards Differential Privacy Analysis in Healthcare AI
authors: Taofik Olajobi, Muhammad Taimoor KHan
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) within healthcare has the potential to bring radical changes in diagnostics, personalized treatment, and operational efficiency. However, the success of this technology depends critically on the ability to access large amounts of patient information, which inevitably raises concerns about privacy on a massive scale. Differential Privacy (DP) has become a formal, mathematically defined framework that provides strong privacy guarantees for individuals in datasets, which is especially important for healthcare AI applications. This review provides a systematic overview of the use, the challenges, and the impact of DP in healthcare. It reviews several DP approaches specific to healthcare, including perturbing gradients during training, synthesizing privacy-preserving datasets, and output perturbation. This work advances a scalable, compliance-ready approach to privacy-preserving healthcare AI by bridging theoretical rigor with practical feasibility. The findings underscore the necessity of moving beyond static DP models toward adaptive, context-aware systems that harmonize patient trust, clinical utility, legal accountability, and prerequisites for the ethical deployment of AI in global healthcare ecosystems.

title: Smart IoT-Enabled Precision Irrigation System with AI-Driven Predictions
authors: S. Parthiban, K. Harini, B. K. Kanya, K. Muthumanickam
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: The agricultural productivity is also influenced by crop diseases, poor application of fertilizers and inappropriate irrigation methods. The non-modern farming practices and early technology-based remedies are based on manual inspection, predetermined irrigation timeframes, or simple sensor-based automation, which are unresponsive and unintelligent. Despite the development of improved machine learning systems and the latest deep learning solutions, most systems are isolated and do not combine the prediction of diseases with the control of the fertilizer and irrigation. In order to overcome these shortcomings, the given system Smart IoTEnabled Precision Irrigation System with AI-Driven Predictions that integrates crop disease detection with fertilizer recommendation, and real-time monitoring of irrigation within the framework of a single system is proposed. The system employs an InceptionNetV3 built deep learning model to classify cropped diseases well based on the leaf image by involves an image of the multi-scale visual features. Depending on the forecasted disease, a smart fertilizer recommendation easily offers recommended treatments to promote the well-being and harvests of crops. Also, the iot-based sensors such as soil moisture and temperature are used for constantly check the field conditions to avoid excessive irrigation and rationalize the use of water. The results of all outputs such as disease prediction, fertilizers and irrigation status are availed through an easy-to-use web interface in order to aid in the making of informed decisions. The suggested system enhances the use of resources, minimizes losses of crops, and encourages sustainable agriculture practices and this makes this system an effective solution in precision farming.

title: Unsupervised Laparoscopy Image Super-Resolution Reconstruction Based on Meta-Contrastive Learning
authors: Xiaoying Pan, Zicheng Lu
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Natural Language Processing (ICNLP)
abstract: Blind super-resolution (SR) is vital for enhancing laparoscopic surgery images, which often suffer from complex, unknown degradations. Existing explicit kernel estimation methods exhibit low inference efficiency, while implicit degradation representation learning often relies on static contrastive strategies that limit their ability to effectively separate image content from degradation information. This paper proposes a MetaContrastive Degradation Representation Aware network (MCDRA) that integrates meta-learning with a dynamic contrastive mechanism. The core innovation is a meta-contrastive learner that dynamically weights positive and negative samples based on input-specific degradation characteristics, enabling intelligent selection of informative pairs for representation learning. A hierarchical meta-training strategy is employed, freezing general feature extractor parameters for stability while optimizing degradation-related parameters via meta-learning to ensure rapid adaptation. Experimental results on laparoscopic images demonstrate that MCDRA outperforms state-of-the-art methods in PSNR and SSIM, particularly in restoring medical-specific degradations like motion blur and Gaussian blur, validating its superior detail recovery and clinical applicability.

title: IKF-AC: Innovation-Based Adaptive Kalman Filtering with Adaptive Compensation Control for High-Temperature Precision Detection and Real-Time Regulation in Electrical Control Cabinets
authors: Yulin Yao, Yungui Luo
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: High-temperature detection and real-time control in electrical control cabinets are critical for industrial reliability, yet existing approaches suffer from three limitations: fixed-covariance Kalman filters diverge above 40 °C under temperature-dependent noise; single-phase fixed-gain controllers cannot simultaneously optimize settling time, overshoot, and disturbance rejection; and standard adaptive filters accumulate estimation bias beyond <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 0.5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$</tex> after <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0 h}$</tex>. We propose IKFAC (Improved Kalman Filter with Adaptive Compensation), integrating: (1) a sliding-window adaptive noise estimator with temperature-dependent covariance scaling; (2) a sigmoid feedforward controller fused with error-normalized nonlinear PID and anti-windup integration; and (3) an EMA residual bias compensator for online drift suppression. Benchmarked against four baselines across six metrics, IKF-AC achieves RMSE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.335^{\circ} \mathbf{C}$</tex>, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$e_{s s}=0.261^{\circ} \mathbf{C}$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$t_{s}=72.6 \mathbf{s}$</tex>, consistently outperforming all competing methods under power fluctuation and sudden load change disturbances.

title: TDSQL-Boundless: A Distributed Database System for Large-scale Heterogeneous Multi-Table Workloads
authors: Yuxing Chen, Jie Jiang, Xiaolong He, Ziyi Lu, Shuo Han, Anqun Pan, Chong Zhu, Chang Liu, Xuan Zhao, Yu Li, Yan Tang, Dongzhi Zhao, Wen Zhang, Hailin Lei, Lixiong Zheng, Feng Zhang, Mingde Zhang, Yiteng Chu, Wei Lu, Yunpeng Chai, Xiaoyong Du

authors: Elli Androulaki, Marcus Brandenburger, May Buzaglo Rosenbaum, Angelo De Caro, Kaoutar Elkhiyaoui, Alexandros Filios, Liran Funaro, Yacov Manevich, Hagar Meir, Senthilnathan Natarajan, Manish Sethi, Yoav Tock

authors: Roque Lopez, Yurong Liu, Christos Koutras, Juliana Freire
