title: Agentic Program Repair from Test Failures at Scale: A Neuro-symbolic approach with static analysis and test execution feedback
authors: Chandra Maddila, Adam Tait, Claire Chang, Daniel Cheng, Nauman Ahmad, Vijayaraghavan Murali, Marshall Roch, Arnaud Avondet, Aaron Meltzer, Victor Montalvao, Michael Hopko, Chris Waterson, Parth Thakkar, Renuka Fernandez, Kristian Kristensen, Sivan Barzily, Sherry Chen, Rui Abreu, Nachiappan Nagappan, Payam Shodjai, Killian Murphy, James Everingham, Aparna Ramani, Peter C. Rigby
abstract:
Aim. With the advent of LLMs, agentic program repair has become viable in large organizations with large codebases. While APR research frequently motivates its techniques around helping developers, Winter et al. [1] report that studies with substantial industrial participants are rare (2%). In this work, we develop an Engineering Agent that fixes source code from test failures at scale across diverse software offerings internally.

Method. Using Llama as the base model, we employ a ReAct harness to build an agent that starts from a test failure triaged by a rule-based test failure bot. The agent operates in an agentic harness with 15 actions (e.g., reading files, searching code, running tests, and generating patches), and it iteratively refines solutions using feedback from static analysis and test execution. After validations pass, we apply an LLM-as-a-Judge to filter low-quality patches before notifying a human reviewer, who can accept and land the change in our monorepo. Because we can progressively roll out the system with layered validation and human review, we use offline benchmarks primarily as readiness gates and complement them with production evaluation based on review and landing outcomes.

Benchmark Findings. We curated offline benchmarks for patch generation, the agent loop, and the LLM-as-a-Judge. We found that a specialized 70B model, internally fine-tuned for patch generation in a search-and-replace format, is highly competitive with a much larger vanilla Llama-405B. We also found that patch format strongly impacts performance, with search-and-replace outperforming unified diff. In ablation studies, symbolic feedback from static analysis and test execution improves the ReAct agent. The balanced configuration achieves a benchmark solve rate of 42.3% using an average 11.8 feedback iterations.

Production Findings. Over a three month period, 80% of the generated fixes were reviewed, of which 31.5% were landed (25.5% of the total number of generated fixes).

Engineer Feedback and Adoption. We used open coding to extract qualitative themes from engineer feedback, including quick approvals, gratitude, and surprise, and we used negative feedback (e.g., test flakiness) to refine the production system. We also analyzed reviewed-but-not merged diffs to understand why validated patches are rejected in practice, including workflow timing, intent mismatches (revert vs. fix-forward), and broader patch acceptability concerns.

title: Enhanced Fake Review Detection in E-Commerce: A Comparative Study of Deep Learning and Hybrid Ensemble Classifiers
authors: Sri Lavanya Sajja, Ravula Pranavi, Thammineni Naga Amulya, Yaramala Aparna, Soudarla Sai Priya, Sudhadi Madhumitha
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: The rise of deceptive “opinion spam” has significantly undermined consumer confidence in the digital marketplace. While existing detection models provide a foundation, they often suffer from a “single-path” limitation—either focusing too heavily on linguistic patterns or relying strictly on user behavior statistics. This paper proposes a dualpath hybrid architecture that bridges this gap. By fusing a finetuned DistilBERT model (designed for deep semantic understanding) with a Logistic Regression classifier (focused on behavioral metadata), we created a more resilient detection system. Our experimental results on 40,433 Amazon reviews demonstrate a top-tier accuracy and F1-score of 93.08%. This hybrid approach not only outperforms traditional single-model methods but also maintains a practical training time of 20.46 seconds, offering a balanced solution for real-world e-commerce security.

title: Evaluating Cross-Architecture Performance Modeling of Distributed ML Workloads Using StableHLO
authors: Jonas Svedas, Nathan Laubeuf, Ryan Harvey, Arjun Singh, Changhai Man, Abubakr Nada, Tushar Krishna, James Myers, Debjyoti Bhattacharjee
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on Performance Analysis of Systems and Software (ISPASS)
abstract: Predicting the performance of large-scale distributed machine learning (ML) workloads across multiple accelerator architectures remains a central challenge in ML system design. Existing GPU and TPU focused simulators are typically architecture-specific, while distributed training simulators rely on workload-specific analytical models or costly post-execution traces, limiting portability and cross-platform comparison. This work evaluates whether MLIR’s StableHLO dialect can serve as a unified workload representation for cross-architecture and crossfidelity performance modeling of distributed ML workloads. The study establishes a StableHLO-based simulation methodology that maps a single workload representation onto multiple performance models, spanning analytical, profiling-based, and simulator-driven predictors. Using this methodology, workloads are evaluated across GPUs and TPUs without requiring access to scaled-out physical systems, enabling systematic comparison across modeling fidelities. An empirical evaluation covering distributed GEMM kernels, ResNet, and large language model training workloads demonstrates that StableHLO preserves relative performance trends across architectures and fidelities, while exposing accuracy trade-offs and simulator limitations. Across evaluated scenarios, prediction errors remain within practical bounds for early-stage design exploration, and the methodology reveals fidelity-dependent limitations in existing GPU simulators. These results indicate that StableHLO provides a viable foundation for unified, distributed ML performance modeling across accelerator architectures and simulators, supporting reusable evaluation workflows and crossvalidation throughout the ML system design process.

title: Immersive Education: A VR Based Classroom Model
authors: Pujari Sailaja, Nithyashree M V, Kuruba Keerthana, Harshitha M C, Manjunath K N
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Virtual Reality (VR) offers new possibilities for self-paced learning by providing immersive environments that support focused study without external distractions. This project presents a VR-Based Self-Learning Classroom developed using Unity, designed to simulate a quiet, structured study space for individual learners. Instead of real- time interaction or multiplayer features, the system provides pre-loaded educational video modules that students can watch inside the virtual classroom. A virtual board is included for taking notes, enabling students to write or view key points while learning. To support doubt clarification, an AI-powered chatbot is integrated into the classroom wall, allowing users to ask subject-related questions and receive instant responses within the VR environment. The objective of this project is to enhance concentration and improve accessibility by offering an immersive, distraction-free learning environment. Initial testing indicates that users experience better focus, smoother content consumption, and convenient access to learning assistance. This work demonstrates how VR can strengthen self-learning systems and provides a foundation for future improvements such as gesture-based note writing, expanded course libraries, and personalized AI tutoring.

title: STOIC-CNN: Strain-Trained Oncovirus-Induced Cancer Prediction Using Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: Aarya Gupta, Julia Kwiek, Sadhana Gangadharan, Charlotte Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: An oncovirus is a virus that has the ability to initiate cancer, often years after initial infection. Oncovirus-associated cancers account for 12% of human cancers globally, contributing to approximately 1.4 million new cases annually. Among the seven widely accepted human oncoviruses: human papillomavirus (HPV), hepatitis B virus (HBV), hepatitis C virus (HCV), Epstein-Barr virus (EBV), Kaposi's sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (KSHV), Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCV), and human T-lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1), there are certain strains that have higher risks for associated cases. Currently, clinical understanding of these associations relies on long-term epidemiological data, limiting real-time response to emerging or mutant strains. In this paper, we propose a deep-learning model that takes a viral genome as input and predicts its associated cancer risks, including type and risk association. Our model maintains high accuracy and ROC-AUC scores even on relatively new strains, which do not have many samples. This Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) approach outlines an approach to enable clinicians and medical professionals to identify high-risk viral strains and could support patient diagnostics, vaccine design, and public health response.

title: Environmental Predictors of Malaria Outbreaks: A Dual-Task Approach With Classification and Regression Modeling
authors: Anvi Kumar, Akshay Murthy, Aryan Tummala, David Wong
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Malaria, a life-threatening disease driven by climatic factors, remains a significant global health challenge where reactive public health responses highlight the critical need for accurate forecasting. This study addresses this need by developing an integrated framework that uses both classification for risk stratification and regression for incidence forecasting from environmental data. A custom dataset was constructed by merging malaria case data from the Malaria Atlas Project with key climate metrics - including temperature, precipitation, humidity, and PM2.5 air quality - for African nations from 2010 to 2020. Machine learning models were trained to (1) classify regions into low, medium, or high-risk categories and (2) predict future case counts. On a hold-out test set for the year 2020, the Random Forest classifier achieved 96.97 % accuracy in risk categorization, while the K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) regression model explained 95 % of the variance <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(R^{2}=0.95\right)$</tex> in incidence rates. The strong performance across both tasks confirms the framework's ability to provide not only risk alerts but also quantitative estimates of outbreak magnitude, offering more actionable intelligence for resource planning than classification alone. The findings demonstrate that this dual approach offers a powerful, scalable tool to aid public health officials in moving from reactive to proactive malaria control.

title: Memory Technology: Enabling the AI Revolution
authors: N. Ramaswamy, A. Calderoni, D. Mocuta, D. Daycock, K. Karda, A. Singh, R. Sreeramaneni
abstract: Rapid growth in Artificial Intelligence (AI) is reshaping system design by driving unprecedented demands for computation, data movement, and power efficiency. As AI models exceed a trillion parameters and workloads focus more on reasoning - memory capacity, bandwidth, latency, and energy efficiency, have become key enablers. To enable disaggregated computing platforms, developments in DRAM scaling, high-bandwidth memory, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.5 \mathrm{D} / 3 \mathrm{D}$</tex> integration technologies-such as hybrid bonding and fine-pitch interconnects-are swiftly progressing. These innovations are driven by parallel breakthroughs in device engineering, materials science, system architecture, packaging, and design. Emerging nonvolatile memories like ferroelectrics also provide opportunities for memory capacity expansion. This paper reviews DRAM and emerging memory innovations vital for advancing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$A I$</tex> in the next decade.

title: Host-Pathogen Interactions: Exploring the Genetic Relationship Between Vesicular Stomatitis Virus and Host mRNA Manipulation in Human Cells
authors: Addisyn Fisher, Ananya Gupta, Jessica Hong, Chloe Koo, Claire Lee, Joonwoo Lee, Ronak Pathak, Luc Reyes, Meredith Levy, Michelle Lin, Michael Royzman, Hrishi Shah, Shaunak Soni, Isabelle Wang, Tyler Dorrity
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Vesicular stomatitis virus (VSV) is a negative-sense RNA virus that infects a wide range of organisms. It is commonly used in modern-day vaccines and research for its wide tropism and limited effect on human health. However, recent research has suggested that VSV may play a role in decreasing the length of the 3' untranslated region (3'UTR) of human messenger ribonucleic acid (mRNA) and lowering immune response. This study found the glycoprotein of VSV (VSV-G gene) to be implicated in this phenomenon, challenging the suitability of VSV to pseudotyping, vaccines, and gene therapies.

title: AgriConnect: A Digital Platform for Real-Time Agricultural News and Scheme Dissemination
authors: T.Sasikala, S.D.Vijayakumar, P. Vijayakumar, P.Vishnu Raja, K Lavanya, Bhuvaneshwari N
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: In India most of the Farmers are struggle to access reliable and updated information about our government schemes and agricultural news. Although we several existing portals, most of them are difficult to navigate or lack of personalisation. In order to fix this issue, we developed an Agricultural News and Schemes Portal, it is a web-based platform and this gives a verified government scheme information with their respective website information, real-time agricultural news, multilingual support, some Indian crop market price and real-time whether in one simple interface. The system uses user preferences like region and crop type to show the relevant content and sends important updates. Overall, the portal supports the Farmers to gain better awareness, making things clearer and gets them involved online and matching the aims of Digital India and Agri-Tech initiatives. Keywords—Agricultural information systems; Government schemes; Farmer support portal; Real-time agricultural news; Multilingual interface; Scheme recommendation; Weather forecasting; Agri-Tech; Digital India; Market price updates; Web-based platform.

title: A Hybrid Stego-Forensic Transformer Technology For End-To-End Image Integrity Protection
authors: Naima George, C. Anitha
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: The security of the digital image integrity should be a harmonious blend of the harmless concealment of in- formation as well as the proper forensic analysis. These are called hybrid stego-forensic learning models which permit the processes of watermark embedding and smart tamper-detecting to be executed simultaneously within the same architecture. A Hybrid Stego-Forensic Transformer (HSFT) is an end-to- end deep learning model that is proposed as a fully automatic image integrity protection system that jointly and simultaneously executes secure data embedding, tamper detection, the location of manipulation, and provenance. It contains a steganographic encoder of adaptive contents and a transformer-based forensic decoder, which allows for the reversibility of the embedding of watermarks with minimal visual degradation. The complexity of local texture has been used to guide a frequency-domain masking approach that enhances payload rate and does not compromise the quality of perception. In the forensic transformer, multi- scale self-attention is utilized to generate fine-grained artifacts and long-range relationships between the image manipulations. The scheme, which includes an adversarial-based hybrid scheme that has perceptual consistency, reconstruction fidelity, and adversarial stability, as well as a localization accuracy, is more robust to compression, scaling, noise, and geometric distortion. The immense testing on the standard benchmark test sets depicts high-quality outcomes in terms of imperceptibility, temporary localization of tampering and reliable authentication, compared to the stego-forensic system at present. The framework offers a scalable and secure application to a solution, which involves trusted visual contents, like digital forensics, surveillance, medical imaging and verification of legal evidence.

title: Cataract Detection Using Machine Learning
authors: Deepika T E, Harsha D S, Bhargavi S M, Baba Fakruddin Ali B H, Raghavendra Patil G E, Mehar Afroz
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Cataract is a leading cause of preventable blindness worldwide, and early detection is critical, particularly in rural and resource-constrained settings. This paper presents a convolutional neural network (CNN)-based cataract detection system capable of classifying eye images as Normal or Cataract and estimating severity levels (Mild, Moderate, Severe). The proposed approach integrates a Tkinter-based graphical user interface (GUI) for real-time webcam screening and image upload. Detailed model architecture, training configuration, preprocessing steps, and evaluation metrics are provided to ensure reproducibility. Experimental results demonstrate strong performance with an overall test accuracy of 94%, competitive with recent state-of-the-art methods. The system is designed to be scalable, low-cost, and suitable for teleophthalmology applications.

title: In-depth energy analysis of a hybrid sMTJ/RRAM Ising machine
authors: H. Levices, M-A. Iftakher, K-E. Harabi, M-C. Faye, P. Talatchian, K. Garello, D. Querlioz, L. Hutin
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: This work investigates an Ising model’s analog in-memory computing implementation for efficiently solving optimization problems. Building on an experimental proof-of-concept hybrid (RRAM + sMTJ) Ising machine, we propose a flexible design and analyze its energy efficiency for various benchmark combinatorial problems. The results illustrate a synchronous low-power system highlighting the potential of co-integrated sMTJ–RRAM hardware as a compact and low-power solution compatible with both bipolar (Ising) and binary (QUBO) formalisms.

title: Enhanced YOLOv13 with Chaotic Wombat Optimization for Plant Leaf Disease Segmentation and Classification
authors: Suganya S, Tamilvizhi T, Surendran R
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Plant leaf disease detection is significant in the precision agriculture for the early and accurate diagnosis that impacts the crop yield, quality and food security. Traditional methods of disease detection and classification model depend on manual inspection methods that are time-consuming, subjective and impractical for large-scale farming. Recent advances in deep learning have significantly improved imagebased plant disease analysis; still, challenges like noise, complex backgrounds, scale variation and suboptimal model convergence limits the performance. To address the issues, this research proposed the plant leaf disease detection and classification model with anisotropic diffusion based preprocessing to enhance leaf image quality by preserving diseaserelevant structures. Then, the proposed Enhanced YOLOv13 (En-YOLOv13) model is employed for precise ROI segmentation, wherein the loss function optimization is employed using the Chaotic Wombat Optimization (CWO) algorithm that incorporates chaotic mapping to enhance the exploration and prevent stagnation in local optima. Finally, the segmented leaf regions are classified using the fine-tuned VGG16 network to identify specific plant diseases. Experimental results on publicly available dataset demonstrated that the proposed model achieves improved segmentation accuracy and makes it suitable for real-world agricultural decision-support systems.

title: KG-Rank-Plus: Enhancing LLM-Based Medical Question Answering with Multi-Hop Knowledge Graph Traversal and Ranking Techniques
authors: Ahmed Tahiru Issah, Idaya Seidu, Emmanuel Amankwaa Adjei, Mona Aman, Azizuddin Ahmed Biyabani
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise in medical question answering but remain prone to hallucinations, especially for complex clinical queries. While KG-Rank addresses this through single-hop knowledge graph integration, its scope is limited for questions requiring multi-hop processing. We present KG-Rank-Plus, an enhanced framework extending KG-Rank through controlled multi-hop traversal using breadth-first search with progressive pruning (20 to 7 to 3 relations per hop) up to three hops. Experiments on LiveQA demonstrate consistent improvements across all ranking methods, with ROUGE-L gain of 0.97 using Similarity Ranking and average BLEURT improve-ment of 5.54 points, highlighting enhanced semantic quality and factual alignment.

title: Automated Tobacco Leaf Grading Using Deep Convolutional Networks and Vision Transformers
authors: M. Rajababu, S. Tejaswin, T. D. N. Vamsi Reddy, S. Durga Bhavani, A. J. Sai Ganesh
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: Cured tobacco has commercial value based on the way it looks and the way it feels. Traditionally, the visual quality of cured tobacco has been tested in a labor-intensive and lowcapacity manual grading process, which often produces inconsistent results when attempting to distinguish subtle differences among samples, especially when the illumination level of the samples varies. This study demonstrates an automated, real-time tobacco leaf grading system that can be used by industry on a large scale. To accomplish this objective, a novel hybrid deep learning architecture comprising both a ResNet50 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for local texture feature extraction and a Vision Transformer (ViT) for global semantic reasoning has been developed and implemented. The dual branch architecture permits both local surface texture analysis of the cured tobacco leaf (i.e., identification of microscopic flaws such as scorch marks or mold) and global color consistency for evaluation of biochemical ripeness. To overcome the important limitation of sample size (less than 400 cured tobacco samples), this study utilized a comprehensive training strategy involving the use of transfer learning from the ImageNet database along with significant, domain-specific data augmentation in the HSV color space, resulting in a 95.2% successful classification rate of the four primary categories of cured tobacco (Mahogany, Yellow, Green, and Blemished) with an average inference time of 35.0ms/leaf. These results suggest that the new hybrid architecture is able to effectively reduce the biases found in prior human-grading processes.

title: Improving the Formability of Magnesium Alloys by Data-Driven Analysis of Solute Additions
authors: Anthony Low, Ignacio Chazarreta, Megan E. Hurley, June Mizak, Yonguk Lee, Victoria M. Miller
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Magnesium alloys have emerged as critical materials for lightweight forged components in the aerospace and automotive industries due to their low density and high specific strength. Poor formability remains a barrier to broader adoption and improving the formability of magnesium alloys requires a thorough understanding of how alloying elements impact deformation, particularly its effect on strain-rate sensitivity. Efforts in enhancing formability have used rare earth elements which promote reorientation of the basal poles during recrystallization. Recent studies suggest alternative additions may emulate the same effects of rare earth elements, improving texture and formability. The current investigation uses compression testing to examine the deformation behavior of multiple binary (Mg-4.5Zn, Mg-6Zn, Mg-9Zn, and Mg-4.5Gd) and commercial magnesium alloys (AZ31B, AJ72E, and ZK60) at varied temperatures and strain rates. It was observed that grain size has a negligible influence on formability, and solute effects are highly temperature-dependent. By comparing experimental data to a thermodynamic model of each alloy, the contribution to formability and mechanical strength of particular intermetallic phases was identified.

title: English and Chinese Semantic Asymmetry between LLM-based Translation and Human Translation
authors: Xueyang Zhang, Zili Chen, Nana Jin
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Natural Language Processing (ICNLP)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) like ChatGPT, based on the “pre-trained model” paradigm, show advanced capabilities in complex tasks, with their translation ability attracting significant attention. Using Appraisal Theory as the analytical framework, this study compares LLM-based and human translations of China’s President Xi Jinping’s New Year speeches, focusing on semantic asymmetry in rendering evaluative meanings. It aims to assess the accuracy of both modalities in conveying source text graduation resources, clarify their differential capabilities in comprehension and high-quality output, and explore the roots of semantic asymmetry. The findings reveal that human translators outperform LLMs in converting “intensification” to “quantification” resources and transforming “isolated” forms into target-language-aligned “infused” forms. They also add more “sharpening” (strong focus) resources to enhance information precision. Human advantages in graduation resource handling lie in cultural and syntactic adaptation, which mitigate semantic asymmetry. While ChatGPT-4o excels in speed and efficiency, it lacks competence in emotional comprehension, cross-cultural nuance, and strategic information highlighting, indicating that LLMs still require enhanced training in these aspects to improve translation quality.

title: Revisiting the RAID Performance Bottleneck in the SSD Era: A Roofline Model Perspective
authors: Jun Zhao, Xiaoyang Wang, Shuo Wang, Congming Gao, Jiwu Shu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Symposium on Performance Analysis of Systems and Software (ISPASS)
abstract: RAID is widely employed in modern storage systems due to its high bandwidth and reliability. While traditional RAID controllers utilize large DRAM caches to mitigate HDD latency, the advent of SSDs has shifted potential bottlenecks to the controller architecture itself. In this paper, we introduce a Roofline model adapted for RAID systems, defining I/O Processing Intensity (IOPI) as the ratio of I/O requests to total bytes transferred. We demonstrate that write performance is constrained by DRAM bandwidth under low IOPI and by computational overhead under high IOPI. To mitigate the DRAM bottleneck, we propose a high-bandwidth SRAM-based architecture, bringing parity-based RAID write performance closer to theoretical limits.

title: Effect of Ozone Annealing on the Characteristics of p-Type SnO TFT
authors: Kai-Sheng Lin, Yu-Ying Lin, Guan-Han Lin, Hsiang-Chao Yang, Tsung-Te Chou, Chien-Wei Chen, Chi-Chung Kei, Fan-Yu Yen, Wei-Yen Chen, Feng-Ching Chu, Feng-Cheng Yang, Chun-Hsiung Lin
publish: 2026 International VLSI Symposium on Technology, Systems and Applications (VLSI TSA)
abstract: In this study, the effect of the ozone treatment on the chemical and electrical characteristics of SnO p-type thin film transistors (TFT) was demonstrated. It was found that the on/off ratio of p-type TFT enhanced by four times after 150 °C O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> annealing. Further increase the annealing temperature induced a p-type to n-type conversion. The devices retain p-type characteristics at O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> annealing temperature of 150-200 °C, while annealing at 250 °C triggers a distinct bipolar conduction, and n-type conduction become dominant with 350 °C annealing. Raman spectra confirm the evolution from SnO to SnO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</inf> phases. XPS analysis further reveal a substantial increase in the Sn<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4+</sup> / Sn<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2+</sup> ratio with higher annealing temperature. These findings highlight that controlled ozone-assisted oxidation offers a tunable pathway for obtaining stable p-type SnO at lower temperatures while inducing bipolar conduction at higher temperatures.

title: TinyLite: Efficient Edge Intelligence for Real-Time Spam Detection Under Device Constraints
authors: Xuanlin Zhu, Johora Akter Polin, Sidi Lu
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Mobility, Operations, Services and Technologies (MOST)
abstract: Modern edge devices increasingly host intelligent services that must operate under strict latency, energy, memory, and thermal constraints while preserving user privacy. Spam detection is a representative example: filtering must occur immediately upon message receipt, often on personal smartphones or IoT-class devices, where sending sensitive content to remote servers introduces both privacy and connectivity concerns. Although Transformer-based classifiers such as BERT deliver strong semantic understanding and state-of-the-art accuracy, their computational cost, often exceeding 100M parameters and billions of FLOPs, makes them difficult to sustain as device-side services. In contrast, traditional lightweight methods such as Naive Bayes and Decision Trees are efficient but brittle under adversarial obfuscation and limited in semantic reasoning. This paper studies spam detection as a deployment-oriented edge intelligence problem and presents TinyLite, a systematically optimized lightweight Transformer for practical on-device inference. We first benchmark six lightweight BERT variants against traditional baselines on SMS, email, and Twitter spam datasets, jointly characterizing classification quality and device-relevant computational cost. We then conduct a capacity sensitivity analysis to understand model utilization relative to task complexity. Guided by these findings, we design TinyLite, a compressed BERT-Tiny derivative that applies attention head pruning and hidden-dimension reduction to achieve 81.6% FLOP reduction while losing less than 1.1 percentage points in F1 score. Finally, we validate TinyLite across continual learning, federated learning, cross-domain transfer, and INT8 quantization scenarios, demonstrating that efficient language intelligence can remain robust under realistic edge deployment conditions.

title: Automated Quality Inspection and Counting Systems for Pharmaceutical Vials: A Technology Review and Future Directions
authors: Geeta Siddramayya Math, Girish V. Kulkarni, Sunith Babu
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Modern pharmaceutical production demands precise quality verification and inventory tracking for glass vials containing injectable medications and vaccines. We examine current research in automated inspection technologies, focusing on deep learning approaches for transparent container quality assessment. Our investigation explores how computer vision techniques handle transparent glass surfaces, what methods researchers use for real-time object tracking on production lines, and where significant technological gaps persist. While numerous studies tackle bottle inspection and conveyor counting separately, we found surprisingly few solutions addressing pharmaceutical vials specifically. Even fewer attempts exist to merge detection, tracking, counting, and quality classification into one cohesive platform. This review highlights promising technologies like YOLO-based detectors, counting and inspection methodologies, along with advanced tracking algorithms, yet reveals that integrating these components for pharmaceutical applications remains an open challenge. We identify specific technical hurdles from handling glass transparency to achieving regulatory compliance that future systems need to overcome to meet industry needs.

title: Neutron Radiation Results of Fault-Tolerant Soft RISC-V Linux SoCs on SRAM-Based FPGAs
authors: Andrew E. Wilson, Nathan G. Baker, Michael Wirthlin
publish: 2025 IEEE Radiation Effects Data Workshop (REDW) (in conjunction with 2025 NSREC)
abstract: This work presents a fault-tolerant RISC-V Linux SoC employing TMR, ECC, and SEU-aware placement on Kintex 7 and KU060 FPGAs. The nuetron radiation results demonstrate improved cross-section upto 43 × for the mitigation methods.

title: Characterization of Several RF Integrated Devices Sensitivity to Total Ionizing Dose
authors: Jean-Christophe Diot, Gilles Assallit, David Poujols
publish: 2025 IEEE Radiation Effects Data Workshop (REDW) (in conjunction with 2025 NSREC)
abstract: Complex RF components are increasingly used. It is therefore essential to be able to characterize their sensitivity to ionizing radiation. The aim of this study is to determine the susceptibility of five RF components: VCO HMC531LP5, PNP BF824215 transistor operating as an amplifier, NPN BFR92 operating as an amplifier, CORVO TQL9062 amplifier and CORVO TQL 9092 amplifier.

title: Optimization of Suction Cup End Effector Parameters for Robotic Motion Complexity
authors: Jennifer U. Nguyen
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: This study investigates the relationship between suction cup end effector parameters and their performance across varying motion complexity levels in robotic pick-and-place operations. Using a six-axis robotic arm equipped with interchangeable suction cups, experiments were conducted across 40 objects with diverse physical properties. Three key suction cup parameters (diameter, number of bellows, and material stiffness) were evaluated against three object characteristics (packaging rigidity, weight, and surface friction) through six predefined motion patterns of increasing complexity. Results demonstrated strong success correlations between cup material stiffness and package rigidity, as well as cup diameter and object weight. Multiple bellows showed particular effectiveness in maintaining seal integrity during complex motions with flexible packaging. The findings highlight the importance of end effector compliance and conformity in achieving reliable manipulation across complex motion patterns, particularly for irregular and flexible packages. This research provides quantitative guidance for suction cup selection in automated handling applications, with implications for improving robotic manipulation for diverse pick applications.

title: Coordinated Industrial Automation with Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning in AIoT Systems
authors: Mfanasibili Ngwenya
abstract: This paper presents a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework for coordinated industrial automation in Artificial Intelligence of Things (AIoT) environments. The framework addresses challenges in decentralized multi-robot systems, including partial observability, communication constraints, and multi-objective optimization for efficiency, safety, and energy use. We model the system as a multi-agent Markov game with decentralized policy learning, graph-based inter-agent communication, and multi-objective reward shaping, implemented using a multi-agent variant of Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO). In a simulated smart warehouse, our system achieves up to 40% faster task completion and 30% lower energy consumption compared to single-agent PPO, MADDPG, and rule-based heuristics. The framework scales to 50 agents while reducing communication overhead by 45%, with emergent cooperative behaviors like dynamic task sharing and congestion avoidance, offering a scalable blueprint for industrial AIoT applications.

title: Real-Time Classification of Human Motion Using PIR Sensors and Deep Learning on Resource Constrained Hardware
authors: Rohan Van Zyl, Johan Schoeman
abstract: Passive Infrared Sensors are a versatile tool in the detection of human motion within an environment. This work demonstrates the feasibility of using time-frequency analysis in combination with deep learning techniques to categorize human motion using Passive Infrared (PIR) sensors in real time, even under significant computational constraints. The proposed system makes use of a Continuous Wavelet Transform and CNN to convert the analogue signal to a time-frequency representation and predict the speed and direction of motion. Over and above the typical motion detection functionality and performance of traditional PIR sensors, this system was able to achieve a speed classification in three categories with an accuracy of 84% at a distance of 3 meters that decreases to 70% for distances further from the sensor. However, the system performed poorly in the prediction of motion direction using the time-frequency method.

title: High-Precision Prediction Model of Low-Altitude Wind Speed Based on CNN-LSTM-GRU Residual Correction
authors: Wenbo Shao, Qing Shi, Hongzhu Xi, Yugang Yin
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: Traditional wind speed prediction models often struggle to accurately capture the non-linearity, non-stationarity, and high-frequency fluctuations of wind speed sequences under complex meteorological conditions. Consequently, they fail to meet the high-precision requirements of emerging low-altitude applications, such as unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) flight and intelligent manufacturing logistics. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a hybrid wind speed prediction model integrating Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, and Gated Recurrent Units (GRU) with a residual correction mechanism. Specifically, the CNN layer extracts local patterns and features from the time series data, which are then learned by the LSTM network to generate an initial wind speed forecast. The residuals between the predicted and actual values are subsequently analyzed by a GRU module to correct future prediction errors, thereby achieving high-precision wind speed estimation. Comparative experiments based on measured low-altitude wind speed data from the CARVE project demonstrate the superiority of the proposed model. Compared to standalone LSTM and hybrid CNN-LSTM models, the proposed method reduces the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) by 30.7% and 21.6%, and the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) by 35.7% and 26.9%, respectively. Furthermore, the Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) is reduced to 5.8729%, and the coefficient of determination <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(R^{2})$</tex> improves to 0.896. These results validate the effectiveness and engineering application value of the proposed model for low-altitude wind speed prediction.

title: Implementation of Infrared and Ultrasonic Sensors to Improve the Accuracy of Parking Space Availability Detection at IoT-Based Rest Areas
authors: Ahmad Fajar Baihaqi, Nilla Rachmaningrum, Dimas Adiputra, Kok Swee Sim
publish: 2026 IEEE 6th International Conference in Power Engineering Applications (ICPEA)
abstract: This research developed an Internet of Things-based parking space availability detection system using infrared and ultrasonic sensors controlled by an ESP32 microcontroller. The system is designed to help drivers quickly find available parking spaces and to help managers monitor parking conditions in real time through Firebase. Testing was conducted on one parking slot with ten different scenarios representing vehicle and non-vehicle conditions at various distances. The test results showed that the infrared sensor had an accuracy and precision of 50 percent and was only effective at medium distances. The ultrasonic sensor performed better with an accuracy of 60 percent and a precision of 57 percent and was able to detect objects up to a distance of 200 cm. Combining both sensors using OR logic improved the reliability of the system compared to infrared sensors alone, but did not provide a significant improvement compared to a single ultrasonic sensor. The results of this study indicate that ultrasonic sensors are more suitable for use in parking detection systems, while system performance can still be improved through optimization of logic and sensor placement.

title: Neuro-Symbolic AI for Legal Knowledge Analysis
authors: P. Kanishka, S. Karthika Sri, R. Poonkuzhali Ramadoss
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: This project presents a Neuro-Symbolic AI tool to intellectually analyze and reason over significant Indian legal texts, such as the Constitution, bills, and case law. The system combines the Neural Part (language parsing and entity extraction with the help of LLMs) and the Symbolic Part (rigorous legal reasoning with the help of logic engines), which allows the system to overcome the restrictions of traditional approaches to AI. The tool converts unstructured legal text to a structured body of knowledge in the form of a knowledge network, making it possible to perform powerful Semantic Search, Question Answering, and Rule-Based Legal Reasoning. It offers the legal community and scholars factual, logical responses and clear explanations. The essence is to provide a consistent, evidence-based interpretation of the law, which will greatly improve the effectiveness and availability of legal research in India.

title: A Damage Diagnosis Method for High-Speed Train Bearings Based on Damage Mechanisms and Transfer Learning
authors: Zhao Jin, Zidi Shan
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: This paper proposes a high-speed train bearing damage diagnosis method based on damage mechanisms and transfer learning. The approach integrates damage mechanisms affecting the outer ring, inner ring, and rolling elements, characterising vibration signal features at different damage locations by extracting time-domain statistical characteristics, frequency-domain energy features, and fault-related frequency indicators (such as BPFO, BPFI, BSF, etc.). A diagnostic framework based on mechanism-driven feature analysis is constructed. Joint Distribution Adaptation (JDA) transfer learning is employed to align features between test bench data and operational data, thereby enhancing the model's diagnostic capability under complex operating conditions. Finally, interpretability analysis validated the consistency between model decisions and physical mechanisms. This approach not only enhances the accuracy of bearing damage diagnosis but also improves the physical interpretability of diagnostic results, providing effective technical support for the health monitoring of high-speed train bearings…

title: A Vision-Guided Adaptive Robotic System for Fabric Gripping
authors: Longjia Sang, Jiang Liu, Tianyu Fu, Rui Song
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: In garment manufacturing automation, handling flexible fabrics presents several core challenges. These include easy deformation, frequent folding, poor robustness in visual localization, and a lack of real-time gripping point planning. To address these issues, this paper proposes a vision-guided robotic fabric gripping system based on YOLO12 and SAM2. First, the system uses the YOLO12 object detection algorithm to quickly localize the cut fabric parts and extract the Region of Interest (ROI). Second, the SAM2 model performs instance segmentation on the ROI to generate a high-precision binary mask of the target fabric. Then, geometric analysis is applied to determine the optimal gripping points for a flexible gripper. This step ensures fabric flatness during transfer and guarantees a fold-free placement. Finally, the system completes the robotic motion planning and executes the fabric transfer. Compared to methods based on finite element simulation and traditional visual matching, this approach significantly improves real-time performance, robustness, and fold-free gripping results. It provides a feasible technical solution for automated fabric handling in flexible garment production lines.

title: Decentralized Multi-Agent Goal Assignment for Path Planning Using Large Language Models
authors: Murad Ismayilov, Edwin Meriaux, Shuo Wen, Gregory Dudek
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Coordinating multiple autonomous agents in shared environments under decentralized conditions is a long-standing challenge in robotics and artificial intelligence. This work addresses the problem of decentralized goal assignment for multiagent path planning, where agents independently generate ranked preferences over goals based on structured representations of the environment, including grid visualizations and scenario data. After this reasoning phase, agents exchange their goal rankings, and assignments are determined by a fixed, deterministic conflictresolution rule (e.g., agent index ordering), without negotiation or iterative coordination. We systematically compare greedy heuristics, optimal assignment, and large language model (LLM)based agents in fully observable grid-world settings. Our results show that LLM-based agents, when provided with well-designed prompts and relevant quantitative information, can achieve near-optimal makespans and consistently outperform traditional heuristics. These findings underscore the potential of language models for decentralized goal assignment in multi-agent path planning and highlight the importance of information structure in such systems.

title: Exploring Emotion Recognition in Informal, User-Generated Social Media Text
authors: Mathews Tladi, Madimetja Jonas Manamela
abstract: Human emotion recognition is a branch of artificial intelligence that can process and/or replicate human emotions with the aim of making man-machine interactions more authentic, rich, and natural. This research aims to enhance the performance of the current text-based emotion recognition systems. The focus of this study will be on the user-generated social media text from the Twitter (X) platform, which includes noise that comes in the form of informal language such as slang, abbreviations, and acronyms that will be used as part of the dataset to develop a BERT-based emotion recognition model. The objective is to decipher and comprehend the characteristics of this noise and learn how to incorporate these noisy and informal elements into emotion recognition systems. The model performance was evaluated using precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy metrics across six emotion categories: anger, disgust, fear, joy, sadness, and surprise. The results demonstrate consistently strong performance, with precision, recall, and F1-scores averaging around 90 percent. The overall model accuracy reached approximately 88 percent, indicating that the BERT-based approach effectively handles noisy and informal Twitter text and performs efficiently across diverse emotional expressions.

title: Coordinated Volt-Var Optimization for a Photovoltaic-Integrated IEEE 33-Bus Distribution System Using Genetic Algorithm and NSGA-II
authors: Sak Srey, Vannak Vai, Kimsrornn Khon, Pagnasatyavorn Sreynattha
publish: 2026 IEEE 6th International Conference in Power Engineering Applications (ICPEA)
abstract: This study introduces a coordinated Volt-Var Optimization (VVO) strategy applied to the IEEE 33-bus distribution system, considering the integration of photovoltaic resources. On-load tap changer (OLTC), capacitor banks, and PV inverters are coordinated using a Genetic Algorithm (GA) for single-objective cases and NSGA-II with Fuzzy decision-making for the multi-objective case. Three objectives are considered: (i) minimizing energy losses, (ii) minimizing OLTC tap operations, and (iii) minimizing energy losses and OLTC tap operations. The approach is tested using realistic PV and load profiles at full, 70%, and 50% loading. The results show that Objective (i) delivers the best technical and economic performance, achieving the lowest energy losses and the highest net income while keeping voltages within 0.95–1.05 p.u. These findings demonstrate that coordinated VVO enhances voltage regulation, reduces technical stress on equipment, and supports reliable high-PV distribution networks.

title: Forecasting Soil Acidification Risk from Wet Deposition Using Deep Learning
authors: Anjali Kapilavai, Edwin Feng
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Soil acidification from atmospheric wet deposition of ammonium threatens crop productivity, biodiversity, and water quality critically. In this study, a deep neural network to predict monthly ammonium wet deposition was introduced and evaluated. The model was applied to the target area of Riverside County, California, from January 2017 to December 2018. Data were averaged for 5 covariates (precipitation, vapor pressure, maximum temperature, minimum temperature, concentration of PM2.5) and for the target, ammonium deposition. Data were resampled to a common 0.01 -degree grid, clipped to the county boundary, and transformed into 632 by 32-pixel patches per covariate with dihedral group D4 augmentations applied to them. Patches were stacked together into multi - band inputs, with corresponding targets of ammonium, and then separated with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 0 \%}$</tex> for training, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5 \%}$</tex> for validation, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5 \%}$</tex> for testing. A model of Attention U-Net was then applied and recast for pixel wise regression tasks. It was trained for 30 epochs with a Huber loss function applied to it. The model achieved a coefficient of determination of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.668,9.60$</tex> of MSE, and 1.54 of MAE. These metrics show the model's potential for high - resolution prediction of wet deposition and support plans to mitigate soil acidification impacts. (Abstract)

title: Stacked Parcel Segmentation Method Based on Improved YOLOv8-Seg
authors: Manqi Shen, Jile Huge, Zhengfa Wen, Dengfan Zhang
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: In terminal-site parcel handling scenarios, constrained space and manual stacking often lead to dense piles and mutual occlusion, resulting in weak inter-instance contact boundaries and pronounced adhesion, which increases the difficulty of instance segmentation and subsequent processing under stacking conditions. To address these issues, this paper proposes an improved YOLOv8-Seg-based segmentation method for stacked parcels at terminal sites. The EMA (Efficient MultiScale Attention) module is introduced into the Neck layer and fused with the C2f structure to form EEC2f (Efficient Multi-Scale Attention-Enhanced C2f), aiming to improve feature quality via adaptive attention while suppressing background interference. In the Head layer, the ASFF (Adaptively Spatial Feature Fusion) module is structurally fused with the original Segment head to form ASFF_Segment. This structure first performs ASFF-based adaptive fusion of P3/P4/P5 to learn the contribution weights of each scale at different spatial locations, and then outputs segmentation masks, thereby improving segmentation accuracy and robustness in terminal-site stacked parcel scenarios. Experimental results show that, compared with the original YOLOv8-Seg, the proposed method improves recall by 2.0%, mAP@ 0.5 by 1.2%, and mAP@ 0.5: 0.95 by 1.0%.

title: An LLM-based Waterway Scheduling Agent Integrated with Semantic Perception and Evolutionary Optimization
authors: Junjie Weng, Mozi Chen, Yiling Ren, Yang Liu, Changzhen Li, Kezhong Liu
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Mobility, Operations, Services and Technologies (MOST)
abstract: The exponential growth of global vessel traffic in restricted waterways poses severe challenges to navigational safety and scheduling efficiency. Traditional manual scheduling suffers from high latency under dynamic pressure, while existing heuristic algorithms often lack the situational awareness required to interpret unstructured Automatic Identification System (AIS) data. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an LLM-driven Waterway Scheduling Agent Framework. The core of this framework is a "numeric-to-semantic" interface that integrates dynamic situational awareness with an underlying Adaptive Genetic Algorithm (AGA). The system features a perception layer that translates physical kinematic deviations into human-readable semantic reports, enabling the LLM to act as a cognitive decision core. Rather than modifying physical coordinates directly, the LLM translates dynamic environmental conditions into adaptive penalty weights for the AGA’s objective function. To manage inherent LLM uncertainties in this safety-critical domain, the framework incorporates a deterministic conflict detection operator as a strict physical guardrail, facilitating a reliable, human-supervised ("human-on-the-loop") collaborative paradigm. Experimental results demonstrate that, compared to manual scheduling, the framework reduces the Total Schedule Adjustment Time from 31.0 hours to 9.5 hours (a 69.4% improvement), validating its capability to transition waterway management from static planning to a reliable, situation-driven adaptive response.

title: Cross-Modal Attention Networks for Illumination-Invariant Gesture Recognition in Human–Computer Interaction
authors: Paul Jeswin G, Ebenezer Lijin S, Kavitha G
publish: 2026 Fourth International Conference on Augmented Intelligence and Sustainable Systems (ICAISS)
abstract: The camera based gesture control usually fails in a dark environment since the feature extraction relies on the contrasts being seen. In order to address this shortcoming, a cross-modal recognition plan is proposed, in which visual frames will be coupled with a second sensory modality to stabilize representation of feature during low-illumination conditions. The framework corrects the brightness of RGB inputs and uses both RGB and depth streams to obtain feature tensors and mixes them by using attention-based alignment. A contrast driven word motivates sample of the same gesture to be near in feature space under bright and dark conditions. Experiments on a publicly available gesture dataset that was adjusted to controlled lighting indicate that accuracy improves by about twenty percentage points in situations below 30 lx recorded by a single stream. The solution enables reliable communication of low-light control boards and indoor assistance. The method can be applied to temporal modeling and run on small devices in the future.

title: KD-EWS: An Explainable AI Framework for Early Warning and Decision Support in Academic Environments
authors: Vinit Kumar Shukla, Seshaiah Merikapudi, Gopinath A R, Aravinda Thejas Chandra, Swetha T, Bhargava R
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: In general institutions still face serious problems accurately detecting learners who are at risk of performing poorly. Though there has been much improvement in the methodology of data-driven decision-making, an important shortcoming that most models possess is being untransparent black boxes. Therefore, this article presents a knowledge-driven early warning system (KD-EWS) as a transparent support tool through extraction of knowledge based on SHAP values, behavioral clustering and stacking ensemble learning methods. Compared to traditional single-model classifiers, the meta-learning architecture in KD-EWS can better handle the typical class imbalances found in educational assessments. On the other hand, experimental results show that the proposed system beats baseline models with an F1-Score of 0.89 and an AUC-ROC of 0.92. Explainable AI (XAI) transforms model outputs into plain, easily grasped intervention tips besides its powers to predict. This inspires & promotes ethics and evidence-based academic rules. KD-EWS is a methodologically sound path for fair academic interventions that fit SDG-4 and the latest educational reforms made by the regulatory by stressing both prediction accuracy and educational gain.

title: Piecewise Hybrid Control of Pulse Frequency Modulation and Phase Shift Modulation for Bidirectional CLLC Converters Over a Wide Output Voltage Range
authors: Yukun Miao, Haiyan Zhang
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Mechatronics Technology and Intelligent Manufacturing (ICMTIM)
abstract: A piecewise PFM/PSM hybrid control strategy for a bidirectional CLLC converter is proposed to improve lowvoltage regulation over a wide output-voltage range. In the proposed method, pure PFM is retained when Vref <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\geqslant 400 \mathrm{V}$</tex>, whereas phase-shift-assisted regulation is introduced only when Vref <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<400 \mathrm{V}$</tex>. Simulation results show that, at 250 V, single PFM drives the switching frequency to its upper limit of about 195 kHz while the output voltage remains around 284 V. In contrast, the proposed method regulates the output to approximately 250 V with a switching frequency of about 188.445 kHz and a phaseshift ratio of about 0.488. At 450 V, both methods show similar operating characteristics and settle near 438.1 V. Dynamic tests across the 400 V threshold further verify smooth mode transition without obvious chattering. Therefore, the proposed method is more suitable as a low-voltage regulation enhancement strategy than as a full-range replacement for conventional PFM.

title: Image and Video Encryption using DNA Quantum Walk Algorithm
authors: Vrinda Shiva Shetty, Prasanna Lakshmi G S, B Shethal, Heera Devi Baral, Nithin Ps, Vidyashree G
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: Because of the development of quantum computation, modern digital infrastructure must meet ever-increasing security standards, which pose serious risks to conventional encryption methods. In this work, stochastic quantum navigation is incorporated into a unique dual-mechanism framework. systems that use genetic molecular encoding procedures to improve multimedia resource protection. For complete protection, the technique makes use of base-level genetic transformation sequences, nonlinear iterative pseudo randomness, and the SHA-256 hashing architecture. utilizing quantum computing in conjunction with the exponential parallel processing found in molecular DNA substrates The platform establishes anti-tampering and end-to-end secrecy maintenance using derived uncertainty principles. The operational viability and performance efficiency are confirmed through field testing using a Python 310 deployment and tooling sets such as OpenCV, NumPy, and Qi skit. Analysis of benchmarking: demonstrates advantages over well-known RSA techniques in terms of data.

title: Phenotyping Using Deep Autoencoders on Instrumented Gait Data of Hemiplegic Subjects
authors: Lakhisha Balaji, Vishal Jonnalagadda, Troy Krupinski, Renugopal Sivaprakasam, Mark V. Albert, Kevin Dibbern
publish: 2025 IEEE MIT Undergraduate Research Technology Conference (URTC)
abstract: Unilateral hemiplegia is the most prevalent form of Cerebral Palsy (CP) among children, affecting <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\sim} \mathbf{1. 5}$</tex> in 1000 live births and causing paralysis on one side of the body. Current clinical classification categorizes hemiplegia subjectively based on the Winters Gage Hicks classification (WGH) but does not effectively characterize subtypes due to visual assessment inconsistencies. Instrumented Gait Analysis (IGA) data generates high-dimensional kinematic data capable of objectively quantifying gait abnormalities in hemiplegic patients. This study aims to develop a standardized framework to examine highdimensional IGA data for the purpose of objectively identifying hemplegic gait patterns through deep-learning and clustering analysis. IGA time series is transformed into 2-dimensional latent space representations through various autoencoder architectures that preserve relevant hemiplegic patterns. The latent space is further compressed through UMAP and t-SNE to capture nonlinear relationships and preserve global structures. K-means clustering was then applied to the latent space to categorize subtypes through five well-defined clusters, and various classifiers were trained on latent-space embeddings. Validation results indicate that a robust autoencoder combined with UMAP yields the most effective latent space representation for hemiplegic gait data, achieving approximately 97% classification accuracy when compared to the WGH classification. These findings support the development of a robust framework that integrates deep autoencoder architectures, dimensionality reduction, clustering, and classification to objectively assess the WGH classification using high-dimensional IGA data.

title: AutoMeet : AI Powered Online Meet Summarizer
authors: Chandana N C, Josy Elsa Varghese, Drupitha Chandrashekar, Keerthana S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Knowledge Engineering and Communication Systems (ICKECS)
abstract: This paper presents AutoMeet:Meeting Assistant, an intelligent system designed to automate post-meeting workflows by transforming raw meeting recordings into structured, actionable insights. The system implements a novel multi-modal processing pipeline: it first performs speaker-aware transcription by diarizing video feeds using Pyannote, OpenCV and Tesseract-based OCR for speaker identification, and transcribing audio with the Whisper ASR model. The extracted text is then processed by a dual-strategy information retrieval engine. A Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) module, built with Sentence Transformers and FAISS, enables context-aware Q&A across meeting transcripts. Simultaneously, a specialized action item extraction module employs both rule-based pattern matching and a local LLM (via Ollama) to identify tasks, assignees, and deadlines with structured JSON output. Identified action items trigger an automated notification workflow, integrating with SMTP to send templated reminder emails. Evaluated on real meeting data, the system demonstrates effective performance with a 81% factual consistency, 74% MRR score in RAG responses, and successful extraction of action items with 100% F1-score. AutoMeet:Meeting Assistant offers a practical, integrated solution to reduce manual overhead in meeting summarization and follow-up coordination, with applications in project management and team productivity.

title: SC-MEC: Spatial-Consistent Motion Error Compensation for Dynamic 3D Imaging
authors: Fuzhong Zhou, Xingfan Chen, Yajun Wang, Qican Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: 3D shape measurement based on phase-shifting profilometry (PSP) has attracted extensive attention due to its high accuracy and non-contact characteristics. However, conventional PSP assumes a static scene during image acquisition, which severely restricts its applicability to dynamic scenarios. To overcome this limitation, this paper proposes a spatial-consistent motion error compensation (SC-MEC) framework for dynamic 3D imaging. Unlike existing methods that model motion-induced distortions solely as phase errors, the proposed approach jointly models pixel mismatch and phase error induced by object motion within a unified spatial framework. The motion vector of the target is estimated directly from reconstructed point clouds at adjacent time steps and, together with the calibrated spatial mapping between image and world coordinates, is used to initialize pixel displacement and phase deviation estimation. Based on this coupled error model, a global optimization strategy using simulated annealing is employed to iteratively refine both pixel-and phase-level compensation parameters by minimizing the geometric discrepancy between reconstructed point clouds.There are three major contributions in our method.Firstly, a spatial consistent motion error compensation (SC-MEC) framework is proposed, which uses the spatial motion information of the target to jointly model the pixel mismatch and phase error, and realizes the robust compensation under the complex non-uniform rigid body motion. Secondly, the motion vectors and compensation parameters are estimated directly from the adjacent reconstructed point clouds, which need not be labeled and are hardware independent, so it has wide applicability. Thirdly, a new optimization paradigm is introduced to solve the coupling error model in structured light measurement, and the simulated annealing algorithm is used to explore the global solution space to more accurately calculate the motion error in structured light measurement.

title: Heterogeneous Integration Photonic Engine Chips: Toward Miniaturized and High-Precision Optical Gyroscopes
authors: Daoxin Sun, Lingyu Wang, Zhizhou Lu, Hongmin Fu, Shijia Fan, Wenxuan Liu, Shan Gao, Yang Pan, Haipeng Yu, Jian Liu, Zhuoheng Ren, Yangchun Guo, Shanshan Du, Li Jin, Junbo Feng, Hongchen Jiao, Hui Li, Wenyuan Xu, Lishuang Feng
abstract: As the demand for high-performance, miniaturized, and low-power devices continues to rise in integrated inertial navigation systems, chip-scale optical gyroscopes have become a central focus for the next generation of navigation sensors. This paper presents an innovative photonic engine chip based on heterogeneous integration technology, successfully implemented in an interferometric optical gyroscope (IFOG) system. The chip achieves high integration of key components—including the light source, coupler, modulator, polarization beam splitter, and photodetector—by combining silicon nitride (SiN) and thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) materials through heterogeneous integration. This approach effectively overcomes the performance limitations inherent in traditional single-material platforms. The chip measures just 2.2mm × 10mm and, after system-level packaging, is seamlessly integrated into the gyroscope system. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed system achieves a bias stability of 0.38°/h, significantly outperforming previously reported comparable technologies and highlighting its strong potential for high-precision inertial sensing applications. This technology not only effectively reduces system size and power consumption but also provides excellent compatibility with CMOS processes and scalability, meeting the rigorous integration and stability requirements of high-precision optical inertial navigation systems. Furthermore, it showcases the feasibility of heterogeneous photonic integration technology in the realm of high-precision inertial sensors, establishing a strong foundation for the realization of true chip-level navigation systems.
